Given: $r = 5 \; \mu\mathrm{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \; \mathrm{m}$, $h = 1 \; \mu\mathrm{m} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \; \mathrm{m}$, $P = 25 \; \mathrm{mmHg}$.

Convert pressure to Pa:

$$P = 25 \times 133.32 = 3333 \; \mathrm{Pa}$$

Compute wall stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{h} = \frac{3333 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.01667}{10^{-6}} = 1.6665 \times 10^{4} \; \mathrm{Pa}$$

Convert to kPa:

$$\sigma \approx 16.67 \; \mathrm{kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma \approx \mathbf{16.67 \; kPa}$

*Commentary:* This low wall stress — despite a thin wall — is why capillaries do not rupture under normal pressure. The small radius compensates for the small thickness.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Property | Arteries | Veins |
|----------|----------|-------|
| Wall composition | Thick, more elastin + collagen | Thin, less elastin, more collagen |
| Radius | Moderate | Slightly larger |
| Wall thickness | Larger | Smaller |
| Compliance ($C$) | **Low (~1.2 mL/mmHg systemic)** | **High (~24 mL/mmHg systemic)** |
| Distensibility ($D$) | Moderate | High |
| Operating pressure | High (80–120 mmHg) | Low (2–8 mmHg) |
| Functional role | Pressure reservoir, conduit, pulse smoothing | Volume reservoir, capacitance |

**Three physiological consequences (2 marks):**

(i) **Hemorrhage compensation:** When blood is lost, veins collapse first because of their low operating pressure. Sympathetic venoconstriction mobilizes the venous reservoir, shifting blood back to the heart. Arteries contribute very little to this refill.

(ii) **Pulse pressure generation:** Arteries, with their moderate compliance, stretch during systole and recoil during diastole — converting pulsatile flow into more continuous flow. As $C_a$ falls with age, pulse pressure widens.

(iii) **Blood reservoir function:** Because total venous compliance is ~20× arterial, the veins hold ~70% of total blood volume. They are the body's principal "blood bank."

**Two factors that alter compliance (1 mark):**

1. **Aging** — progressive fragmentation of elastin, increased collagen deposition, and calcification → reduced arterial compliance.
2. **Chronic hypertension** — wall hypertrophy and remodeling → reduced compliance (arterial side).
3. *Others accepted:* Sympathetic tone (acute venoconstriction reduces unstressed volume), atherosclerosis, diabetes mellitus, physical training (increases compliance).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic — and where you lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units.** "Compliance = 0.6" is **wrong**; it must be "0.6 mL/mmHg." Examiners routinely deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing compliance and distensibility.** They are *not* the same. Compliance is size-dependent; distensibility is not. Many students write $C = D / V$ instead of $C = D \cdot V$. Remember: $C = D \cdot V$, derived from $D = (\Delta V / V) / \Delta P$.
> 3. **Wrong Laplace formula.** Some students write $\sigma = P \cdot r$ (this is the *tension* per unit length, not the wall *stress*). For stress you **must** divide by wall thickness $h$.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\Delta P$ and $\Delta V$.** Always take $P_2 - P_1$ as the final minus initial; reversing the order yields a negative compliance which is meaningless.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps.** In 7-mark derivations, the examiner awards at most 1 mark for the final boxed answer. You must show the force balance explicitly.
> 6. **Wrong Windkessel statement.** The capacitor $C$ is in **parallel** with $R$ (not in series). Drawing them in series is a structural error worth 2 marks.
> 7. **Treating sphere and cylinder the same.** Wall stress in a sphere is $Pr / 2h$, half the cylindrical value. Examiners test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Compliance $C = \Delta V / \Delta P$** — extrinsic property, unit **mL/mmHg** or **m³·Pa⁻¹**.
- **Distensibility $D = (\Delta V / V) / \Delta P$** — intrinsic material property, unit **per mmHg** or **per Pa**.
- **Key identity:** $C = D \cdot V$ (compliance = distensibility × volume).
- **Elastance** (stiffness) $= 1 / C$.
- **Geometric formula:** $C = 2 \pi r^3 L / (E h)$ — compliance rises with the **cube** of radius, falls with stiffness and thickness.
- **Laplace's law (cylinder):** $T = P r$, $\sigma = P r / h$. **Sphere:** $\sigma = P r / (2 h)$.
- **Parallel compliances add; series compliances add as reciprocals.**
- **Veins are the capacitance reservoir** — total venous compliance ≈ **24 mL/mmHg** vs. arterial ≈ **1.2 mL/mmHg**; veins hold **~70%** of blood volume.
- **Windkessel two-element model:** $C$ (aortic compliance) in parallel with $R$ (peripheral resistance); time constant $\tau = R C$.
- **Pulse pressure** $\Delta P_p \approx SV / C_a$ — *inversely* related to arterial compliance.
- **Aortic stiffening** (aging, hypertension, diabetes) → reduced $C_a$ → wider pulse pressure → isolated systolic hypertension.
- **Capillary wall stress is low** because $r$ is small (Laplace's law protects thin walls).
- **Sympathetic venoconstriction** reduces venous compliance acutely, shifting blood to the arterial side.
- **Hemorrhage tolerance** is provided by the venous reservoir, not the arterial tree.
- **Examiners love** numerical problems combining $C$, $D$, Laplace's law, and the Windkessel model in a single question — practice all four.
- **Remember to state units in every numerical answer** — the most common, easiest-to-fix mark-loss point in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blood Consists of Cells Suspended in Plasma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT415 - QUANTITATIVE PHYSIOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Blood Consists of Cells Suspended in Plasma # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Blood** is a specialized **connective tissue** composed of a **liquid extracellular matrix (plasma)** in which **formed elements (cells and cell fragments)** are suspended. It circulates within the cardiovascular system under hydrostatic pressure generated by the heart, functioning as the body's principal **transport medium**, **homeostatic regulator**, and **defense system**. In quantitative physiology, blood is modeled as a **two-phase fluid suspension**: - **Phase 1 — Plasma** (the continuous aqueous phase, ~**55%** of total blood volume). - **Phase 2 — Formed Elements** (the dispersed cellular phase, ~**45%** of total blood volume), which include: 1. **Erythrocytes (Red Blood Cells / RBCs)** — ~**99%** of formed elements by count. 2. **Leukocytes (White Blood Cells / WBCs)** — five sub-types. 3. **Platelets (Thrombocytes)** — cytoplasmic fragments of megakaryocytes. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** > The student must be able to: > 1. State the volumetric fractions of plasma and formed elements. > 2. Identify the cellular and non-cellular constituents of whole blood. > 3. Quantitatively express the **hematocrit** and interpret its clinical range. > 4. Describe the differential composition of plasma (water, proteins, solutes). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River-Cargo Analogy" Imagine blood as a **busy river** flowing through a network of canals (blood vessels): - The **river water** = **Plasma** (the liquid medium, ~91–92% water itself, carrying dissolved cargo like nutrients, gases, proteins, hormones, and waste). - The **boats and barges floating on the river** = **Formed Elements** (RBCs, WBCs, Platelets). - **Cargo barges (RBCs)** are the most numerous — they pick up oxygen at the lungs (loading dock) and deliver it to tissues (unloading docks). - **Patrol boats (WBCs)** are fewer but essential — they patrol for invaders (pathogens). - **Repair crews (Platelets)** are tiny fragments that rush to seal leaks in the canal walls when damage occurs. This analogy helps visualize that **blood is not just a homogeneous red liquid** — it is a **structured suspension** whose physical and functional behavior emerges from the *interaction* between the liquid plasma and the suspended cells. --- ## 1.3 Quantitative Snapshot of Blood | Parameter | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Total blood volume (adult) | **~5.0–5.5 (males)**, **~4.0–4.5 (females)** | Liters (L) | | Plasma volume fraction | **~55%** (0.55) | fraction | | Formed elements volume fraction | **~45%** (0.45) | fraction | | **Hematocrit (Hct)** | **~45% (males)**, **~41% (females)** | % packed cell volume | | Blood pH | **7.35 – 7.45** | pH units | | Blood osmolality | **~285–295** | mOsm/kg H₂O | | Specific gravity of whole blood | **~1.050–1.060** | dimensionless | | Specific gravity of plasma | **~1.025–1.030** | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > The clinically measured **hematocrit (Hct)** and the **volume fraction of all formed elements** are slightly different: Hct strictly measures *only* the RBC fraction (after centrifugation), but because RBCs dominate the cellular phase, Hct ≈ **~45%** is used as a practical surrogate for the *total* formed-element fraction (~45%). > The **3% gap** (~45% vs. ~48% in some texts) arises because Hct does **not** account for trapped plasma between RBCs in the centrifuged column. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Phase 1 — Plasma (The Liquid Matrix) Plasma is the **straw-colored, protein-rich aqueous medium** (≈ 91–92% water) in which formed elements are suspended. It is the *transport vehicle* for nearly everything that must move between organs. ### 2.1.1 Composition of Plasma (by mass) | Component | Approximate % | Key Sub-components / Examples | |---|---|---| | **Water** | **91 – 92 %** | Solvent for all plasma solutes | | **Plasma Proteins** | **7 – 8 %** | Albumin (~60%), Globulins (~36%), Fibrinogen (~4%) | | **Other Solutes** | **1 – 2 %** | Electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), nutrients (glucose, amino acids, lipids), gases (O₂, CO₂, N₂), hormones, vitamins, waste products (urea, creatinine, uric acid, bilirubin) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Big Three Plasma Proteins — Roles to Remember for KTU:** > - **Albumin** (~3.5–5.0 g/dL): Generates **colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure**; binds and transports hormones, drugs, fatty acids. > - **Globulins** (~2.0–3.5 g/dL): Includes **α-, β-, γ-globulins**; γ-globulins are **immunoglobulins (antibodies)**. > - **Fibrinogen** (~0.2–0.4 g/dL): Essential **clotting factor**; converted to fibrin during coagulation. ### 2.1.2 Why Plasma Is More Than "Just Water" Plasma proteins cannot freely cross the capillary endothelium, while water and small ions can. This asymmetry creates the **Starling forces** that govern fluid exchange across capillary walls: - **Hydrostatic pressure** (pushes fluid *out*). - **Colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure π** (pulls fluid *in*). The **oncotic pressure of plasma** is dominated by albumin: $$\pi \approx 2.1 \cdot c + 0.16 \cdot c^2 + 0.009 \cdot c^3 \quad \text{(Landis–Pappenheimer, in mmHg; c in g/dL)}$$ For typical albumin concentration $c \approx 4$ g/dL, $\pi \approx 25$ mmHg. ### 2.1.3 Serum vs. Plasma — A Subtle but KTU-Favorite Distinction - **Plasma** = whole blood *minus* the cells, *with* clotting factors intact (obtained by adding anticoagulant and centrifuging). - **Serum** = plasma *minus* fibrinogen and other clotting factors (obtained by allowing blood to clot, then centrifuging). > [!NOTE] > This distinction is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU university exams. --- ## 2.2 Phase 2 — The Formed Elements (Cellular Suspension) ### 2.2.1 Red Blood Cells (Erythrocytes) | Property | Value | |---|---| | Count (males) | **4.5 – 5.5 × 10⁶ / μL** | | Count (females) | **4.0 – 5.0 × 10⁶ / μL** | | Diameter | **~7.5 μm** | | Thickness (rim) | **~2.0 μm** | | Surface area (total) | **~3,800 m²** (all RBCs combined) | | Shape | **Biconcave disc** (high surface-area-to-volume ratio) | | Lifespan | **~120 days** | | Site of production | **Red bone marrow** (myeloid lineage) | | Site of destruction | **Spleen** (and liver) | | Key protein | **Hemoglobin (Hb)** | | Normal Hb | **13.5–17.5 g/dL (males)**, **12.0–15.5 g/dL (females)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > Erythrocytes are the **only human cells without a nucleus** at maturity. This maximizes intracellular space for hemoglobin and improves deformability through capillaries (which can be as narrow as **5 μm**). ### 2.2.2 White Blood Cells (Leukocytes) Leukocytes are the **nucleated defense cells** of blood, classified into two lineages: **A. Granulocytes (cytoplasmic granules visible on staining):** 1. **Neutrophils (50–70%)** — first responders to bacterial infection; phagocytosis. 2. **Eosinophils (1–4%)** — combat parasites; mediate allergic reactions. 3. **Basophils (0.5–1%)** — release histamine and heparin in inflammation. **B. Agranulocytes (no prominent granules):** 4. **Lymphocytes (20–40%)** — T cells, B cells, NK cells; adaptive immunity. 5. **Monocytes (2–8%)** — largest leukocytes; mature into macrophages in tissues. | Property | Value | |---|---| | Total WBC count | **4,000 – 11,000 / μL** | | Differential count | See percentages above | ### 2.2.3 Platelets (Thrombocytes) | Property | Value | |---|---| | Count | **150,000 – 400,000 / μL** | | Diameter | **2 – 4 μm** | | Origin | **Cytoplasmic fragments** of megakaryocytes (bone marrow) | | Lifespan | **~8–10 days** | | Primary function | **Hemostasis** — plug formation and clot stabilization | --- ## 2.3 The Hematocrit — The Single Most Important Number in Hematology **Hematocrit (Hct)** is defined as the **volume fraction of RBCs** in whole blood, expressed as a percentage or decimal. $$Hct = \frac{V_{RBC}}{V_{whole\,blood}} \times 100\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The term comes from Greek *haima* (blood) + *krinein* (to separate). After centrifugation, RBCs form the bottom packed layer; plasma the top straw-colored layer; a thin **buffy coat** (WBCs + platelets) sits at the interface. ### 2.3.1 What the Centrifuge Tube Shows ``` ┌──────────────┐ ← Plasma top layer (~55%) — straw-yellow │ │ ↳ Buffy coat (WBCs + Platelets) < 1% ├──────────────┤ ← Interface │ │ │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ ← Packed RBCs (Hct ≈ 45%) │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ └──────────────┘ ``` ### 2.3.2 Clinical Significance of Hematocrit - **Low Hct** → **Anemia** (↓ RBCs, ↓ Hb, ↓ O₂ carrying capacity). - **High Hct** → **Polycythemia** (↑ RBCs, ↑ blood viscosity, ↑ risk of thrombosis). - **High altitude dwellers / athletes** often show elevated Hct due to EPO-driven erythropoiesis. - **Hydration status** also affects Hct (dehydration → ↑ Hct; overhydration → ↓ Hct). --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hematocrit (fraction) | $Hct = V_{RBC}/V_{blood}$ | **0.41 – 0.53** | | 2 | Plasma volume fraction | $f_p = 1 - Hct$ | **~0.55** | | 3 | Total blood volume | $V_{blood} \approx 70 \, mL/kg$ body wt | **~5 L (adult)** | | 4 | Plasma volume | $V_{plasma} = V_{blood} \cdot (1 - Hct)$ | **~3 L** | | 5 | RBC volume | $V_{RBC} = V_{blood} \cdot Hct$ | **~2 L** | | 6 | Mean Corpuscular Volume | $MCV = (Hct\,[\%] \times 10) / RBC\,[\times 10^6/\mu L]$ | **80 – 100 fL** | | 7 | Mean Corpuscular Hb | $MCH = (Hb\,[g/dL] \times 10) / RBC\,[\times 10^6/\mu L]$ | **27 – 33 pg** | | 8 | Mean Corpuscular Hb Conc. | $MCHC = Hb\,[g/dL] / Hct\,[\%]$ | **32 – 36 g/dL** | | 9 | Plasma oncotic pressure (albumin-driven) | $\pi \approx 25$ mmHg (at 4 g/dL albumin) | **mmHg** | | 10 | Blood viscosity relative to water | $\eta_{rel} \approx 3 - 4$ (at high shear) | **dimensionless** | > [!NOTE] > The three "RBC indices" — MCV, MCH, MCHC — are **high-yield KTU 2–3 mark favourites** because they classify anemias (microcytic / normocytic / macrocytic; hypochromic / normochromic). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility The composition of blood as a **cell suspension in plasma** has direct engineering implications: 1. **Blood is a non-Newtonian fluid** — viscosity depends on shear rate (Hct-dependent). Critical for designing **extracorporeal circulation pumps**, **dialysis machines**, and **heart-lung bypass systems**. 2. **Hematocrit governs oxygen-carrying capacity and viscosity trade-off.** Devices that measure Hct include **centrifugal microhematocrit readers** and **automated impedance / flow-cytometry hematology analyzers**. 3. **Plasma separation by centrifugation** is the foundation of **blood-banking technology** (component therapy: packed RBCs, fresh frozen plasma, platelet concentrates). 4. **Biosensor and lab-on-chip** designs for point-of-care diagnostics rely on knowing plasma-to-cell ratios to calibrate optical (e.g., absorbance at 540 nm for hemolysis) and electrical (impedance spectroscopy) measurements. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Blood Volume from Body Weight The total circulating blood volume in a healthy adult is empirically approximated as: $$V_{blood} \approx 70 \; \text{mL/kg body weight}$$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** 1. The body must perfuse every tissue continuously; total blood volume scales with metabolic mass. 2. Empirically, ~70 mL of blood is required per kg of body weight for adequate perfusion. 3. For a typical adult of mass $M$ kg: $$V_{blood} = 70 \times M \quad \text{[mL]}$$ For $M = 70$ kg: $$V_{blood} = 70 \times 70 = 4900 \; \text{mL} \approx 5 \; \text{L}$$ 4. **Note on gender variation:** Adult females average slightly lower per-kg blood volume (~65 mL/kg), due to higher adiposity (fat tissue is less vascular). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Plasma and RBC Volumes from Hct > **Problem:** A male patient has body weight 80 kg and a measured hematocrit of 0.48. Calculate (a) total blood volume, (b) plasma volume, (c) total RBC volume, (d) the percentage of his blood that is *not* cellular. ### Solution **(a) Total blood volume:** $$V_{blood} = 70 \times 80 = 5600 \; \text{mL}$$ **(b) Plasma volume:** $$V_{plasma} = V_{blood} \cdot (1 - Hct) = 5600 \times (1 - 0.48) = 5600 \times 0.52 = 2912 \; \text{mL}$$ **(c) RBC volume:** $$V_{RBC} = V_{blood} \cdot Hct = 5600 \times 0.48 = 2688 \; \text{mL}$$ **(d) Non-cellular fraction:** $$1 - Hct = 1 - 0.48 = 0.52 = \mathbf{52\%}$$ > **Sanity check:** Plasma + RBC = 2912 + 2688 = 5600 mL = $V_{blood}$. ✓ > Note that the WBC + platelet contribution (~1%) is typically folded into the plasma fraction in clinical Hct readings. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) > **Problem:** A patient's lab report shows: Hb = 9 g/dL, RBC = 3 × 10⁶/μL, Hct = 30%. Calculate MCV, MCH, and MCHC. Interpret the results. ### Solution **(i) MCV** (average volume of one RBC): $$MCV = \frac{Hct\,[\%] \times 10}{RBC\,[\times 10^6/\mu L]} = \frac{30 \times 10}{3} = 100 \; \text{fL}$$ **(ii) MCH** (average Hb content per RBC): $$MCH = \frac{Hb\,[g/dL] \times 10}{RBC\,[\times 10^6/\mu L]} = \frac{9 \times 10}{3} = 30 \; \text{pg}$$ **(iii) MCHC** (average Hb concentration per unit RBC volume): $$MCHC = \frac{Hb\,[g/dL]}{Hct\,[\%]} = \frac{9}{30} = 0.30 \; \text{g/dL} = \mathbf{30 \; g/dL}$$ ### Interpretation Matrix | Index | Value | Reference Range | Classification | |---|---|---|---| | MCV | 100 fL | 80 – 100 fL | **Normocytic** (normal size) | | MCH | 30 pg | 27 – 33 pg | **Normochromic** (normal Hb content) | | MCHC | 30 g/dL | 32 – 36 g/dL | **Slightly hypochromic** | > [!NOTE] > **Clinical takeaway:** A low Hb (9 g/dL) with normal MCV/MCH suggests a **normocytic-normochromic anemia** — typical of acute blood loss, anemia of chronic disease, or aplastic anemia. In contrast, **iron-deficiency anemia** would show **microcytic hypochromic** indices (low MCV, low MCH, low MCHC). --- ## 3.4 Quantitative Relationship — Hematocrit, Viscosity, and O₂ Delivery Blood viscosity $\eta_b$ depends strongly on Hct. An empirical relationship (Pirofsky / Chien) is: $$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta_{blood}}{\eta_{plasma}} \approx 1 + 2.5 \cdot Hct$$ This is the **Einstein relation** for dilute suspensions, modified for deformable RBCs. ### Worked Example > **Problem:** If plasma viscosity is 1.2 cP, calculate (a) relative viscosity and (b) absolute blood viscosity for a patient with Hct = 0.45. **(a)** $$\eta_{rel} \approx 1 + 2.5 \times 0.45 = 1 + 1.125 = 2.125$$ **(b)** $$\eta_{blood} = \eta_{rel} \times \eta_{plasma} = 2.125 \times 1.2 = 2.55 \; \text{cP}$$ > **Clinical note:** Although increasing Hct increases O₂ carrying capacity linearly, **viscosity rises faster**, so total O₂ delivery (which depends on flow rate × O₂ content) peaks near an Hct of **~0.30 – 0.35** at rest. --- ## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix | Layer | Component | Volume / Count | Key Function | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---|---|---| | Plasma | Water | ~91–92% of plasma | Solvent | "Liquid matrix" | | Plasma | Albumin | ~3.5–5.0 g/dL | Oncotic pressure, transport | "Colloid osmotic pressure" | | Plasma | Globulins | ~2.0–3.5 g/dL | Immunity (γ), transport (α, β) | "Immunoglobulins" | | Plasma | Fibrinogen | ~0.2–0.4 g/dL | Clotting | "Clotting factor" | | Cells | RBCs | ~5 × 10⁶/μL | O₂ / CO₂ transport | "Erythrocytes, biconcave" | | Cells | WBCs | ~7 × 10³/μL | Immunity, phagocytosis | "Leukocytes, differential count" | | Cells | Platelets | ~2.5 × 10⁵/μL | Hemostasis | "Thrombocytes" | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Composition of Whole Blood ```mermaid flowchart TD A["WHOLE BLOOD
~5 Liters"]:::root --> B["PLASMA ~55 percent"]:::plasma A --> C["FORMED ELEMENTS ~45 percent"]:::cells B --> B1["WATER 91 to 92 percent"]:::water B --> B2["PLASMA PROTEINS 7 to 8 percent"]:::protein B --> B3["OTHER SOLUTES 1 to 2 percent"]:::solute B2 --> B2A["ALBUMIN ~60 percent"]:::alb B2 --> B2B["GLOBULINS ~36 percent"]:::glob B2 --> B2C["FIBRINOGEN ~4 percent"]:::fib B2A --> B2A1["Oncotic pressure"] B2A --> B2A2["Hormone drug transport"] B2B --> B2B1["Alpha globulins transport"] B2B --> B2B2["Beta globulins transport"] B2B --> B2B3["Gamma globulins antibodies"] B2C --> B2C1["Clotting cascade"] B3 --> B3A["Electrolytes Na K Cl HCO3"] B3 --> B3B["Nutrients glucose amino acids"] B3 --> B3C["Gases O2 CO2 N2"] B3 --> B3D["Hormones and wastes"] C --> C1["ERYTHROCYTES ~99 percent of cells"]:::rbc C --> C2["LEUKOCYTES less than 1 percent"]:::wbc C --> C3["PLATELETS less than 1 percent"]:::plt C1 --> C1A["Biconcave disc"] C1 --> C1B["Anuclear mature"] C1 --> C1C["Hemoglobin carrier"] C1 --> C1D["Lifespan 120 days"] C2 --> C2A["Neutrophils 50 to 70 percent"] C2 --> C2B["Lymphocytes 20 to 40 percent"] C2 --> C2C["Monocytes 2 to 8 percent"] C2 --> C2D["Eosinophils 1 to 4 percent"] C2 --> C2E["Basophils 0.5 to 1 percent"] C3 --> C3A["Megakaryocyte fragments"] C3 --> C3B["Lifespan 8 to 10 days"] C3 --> C3C["Hemostasis clot formation"] classDef root fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1a252f,stroke-width:2px classDef plasma fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f6391,stroke-width:2px classDef water fill:#85c1e9,color:#000000,stroke:#3498db classDef protein fill:#48c9b0,color:#000000,stroke:#117864 classDef solute fill:#a3e4d7,color:#000000,stroke:#117864 classDef alb fill:#f7dc6f,color:#000000,stroke:#b7950b classDef glob fill:#f5b041,color:#000000,stroke:#9c640c classDef fib fill:#e59866,color:#000000,stroke:#a04000 classDef cells fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px classDef rbc fill:#ec7063,color:#ffffff,stroke:#922b21 classDef wbc fill:#bb8fce,color:#ffffff,stroke:#6c3483 classDef plt fill:#f1948a,color:#000000,stroke:#922b21 ``` ## 4.2 Centrifugation Result — Hematocrit Tube ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph tube[Centrifuge Tube Cross Section] direction TB T1["PLASMA LAYER
Straw yellow ~55 percent
Contains dissolved proteins nutrients hormones wastes"]:::plasma_top T2["BUFFY COAT
WBCs and Platelets less than 1 percent
Thin whitish layer"]:::buffy T3["PACKED RBC LAYER
Dark red Hct ~45 percent
Bottom packed erythrocyte column"]:::rbcpack end T1 --- T2 T2 --- T3 classDef plasma_top fill:#f9e79f,color:#000000,stroke:#7d6608 classDef buffy fill:#fdf2e9,color:#000000,stroke:#935116 classDef rbcpack fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#641e16 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Plasma ↔ Cells Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PHASE1[PHASE 1 PLASMA] direction TB P1["Transport Medium"]:::pp P2["Oncotic Pressure Regulator Albumin"]:::pp P3["Immune Surveillance Globulins"]:::pp P4["Clotting Cascade Fibrinogen"]:::pp end subgraph PHASE2[PHASE 2 FORMED ELEMENTS] direction TB F1["Erythrocytes Gas Exchange"]:::rr F2["Leukocytes Defense and Immunity"]:::ww F3["Platelets Hemostasis"]:::tt end subgraph FUNCTIONS[DOWNSTREAM PHYSIOLOGY] direction TB D1["Tissue Perfusion"]:::dd D2["Homeostasis"]:::dd D3["Immune Defense"]:::dd D4["Wound Repair"]:::dd end P1 --> F1 P2 --> F1 P2 --> D2 P3 --> F2 P3 --> D3 P4 --> F3 F1 --> D1 F2 --> D3 F3 --> D4 classDef pp fill:#d6eaf8,color:#000000,stroke:#1f618d classDef rr fill:#fadbd8,color:#000000,stroke:#922b21 classDef ww fill:#d7bde2,color:#000000,stroke:#6c3483 classDef tt fill:#f5cba7,color:#000000,stroke:#935116 classDef dd fill:#d4efdf,color:#000000,stroke:#196f3d ``` ## 4.4 Erythrocyte Biconcave Disc — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Bone Marrow Stem Cell"]:::stem --> B["Erythroblast Nucleated"]:::ebl B --> C["Reticulocyte Enucleated in blood"]:::ret C --> D["Mature Erythrocyte Biconcave Anuclear"]:::mature D --> E["Circulate 120 days"]:::circ E --> F["Spleen Old RBC Phagocytosis"]:::spleen F --> G["Iron Recycled to Marrow"]:::recycle D --> D1["Maximal Hb packing 250 million per cell"]:::prop D --> D2["High SA to V ratio for gas exchange"]:::prop D --> D3["Deformable through 5 micron capillaries"]:::prop classDef stem fill:#fdebd0,color:#000000,stroke:#935116 classDef ebl fill:#fad7a0,color:#000000,stroke:#7e5109 classDef ret fill:#f8c471,color:#000000,stroke:#7e5109 classDef mature fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#641e16 classDef circ fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#641e16 classDef spleen fill:#7b241c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b2631 classDef recycle fill:#1abc9c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#117a65 classDef prop fill:#f5b7b1,color:#000000,stroke:#922b21 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]** **Define hematocrit. State its normal range in healthy adult males and females. List TWO conditions in which hematocrit is abnormally elevated.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Hematocrit (Hct) is the **volume percentage of red blood cells (erythrocytes) in whole blood**, measured after centrifugation of a capillary tube filled with anticoagulated blood. $$Hct = \frac{V_{RBC}}{V_{whole\,blood}} \times 100\%$$ **Normal Range:** - Adult males: **40 – 54%** (typical ~45%) - Adult females: **36 – 48%** (typical ~41%) **Two conditions with elevated Hct:** 1. **Polycythemia vera** — a myeloproliferative disorder causing RBC overproduction. 2. **Chronic hypoxia** (e.g., high-altitude dwellers, COPD) — ↑ EPO → ↑ RBC production. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Differentiate between plasma and serum. Why is serum preferred over plasma for many biochemical assays?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Plasma | Serum | |---|---|---| | Obtained by | Centrifuging anticoagulated blood | Centrifuging clotted blood | | Contains clotting factors | **Yes** (all, including fibrinogen) | **No** (fibrinogen consumed during clotting) | | Contains fibrinogen | Yes | No | | Volume relative to blood | ~55% of whole blood | Slightly less than plasma (clot occupies volume) | | Color | Straw-yellow | Straw-yellow | | Use | Coagulation studies, therapeutic plasma exchange | Most biochemical/immunological assays | **Why serum is preferred for biochemical assays:** - Clotting has already been completed, so no further enzymatic reactions (e.g., continued glucose consumption by residual cells) alter analyte concentrations. - Cleaner matrix with no anticoagulant interference (e.g., EDTA, heparin, citrate can inhibit enzymatic assay reagents). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Choice A]** **(a)** Describe the composition of blood with a neat labeled diagram. Enumerate the formed elements and state the normal count of each per microliter. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the three major plasma proteins, their normal concentrations, and their physiological roles. How does albumin generate colloid osmotic pressure, and what is its clinical significance? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer to (a):** Blood is a **specialized connective tissue** consisting of a **liquid extracellular matrix (plasma, ~55%)** in which **formed elements (~45%)** are suspended. **Composition Table:** | Component | % Volume | Sub-components | |---|---|---| | Plasma | ~55% | Water (91–92%), Proteins (7–8%), Solutes (1–2%) | | Formed Elements | ~45% | RBCs (~99%), WBCs (<1%), Platelets (<1%) | **Formed Element Counts:** | Cell Type | Normal Count | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | Erythrocytes (RBCs) | **4.5 – 5.5 × 10⁶ / μL** (males); **4.0 – 5.0 × 10⁶ / μL** (females) | O₂ and CO₂ transport via Hb | | Leukocytes (WBCs) — total | **4,000 – 11,000 / μL** | Defense and immunity | | Neutrophils | 50 – 70% of WBCs | Phagocytosis of bacteria | | Lymphocytes | 20 – 40% of WBCs | Adaptive immunity (T, B, NK cells) | | Monocytes | 2 – 8% of WBCs | Phagocytosis, antigen presentation | | Eosinophils | 1 – 4% of WBCs | Anti-parasitic, allergy | | Basophils | 0.5 – 1% of WBCs | Histamine, heparin release | | Platelets | **150,000 – 400,000 / μL** | Hemostasis, clot formation | **Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** (Hierarchical Composition) — show whole blood branching into plasma and formed elements with sub-components labeled. **Key functional points:** - RBCs are **biconcave, anuclear** cells — maximizes surface area and Hb content. - The **buffy coat** (WBCs + platelets) is < 1% but clinically critical. - Plasma serves as the **transport medium** for nutrients, gases, hormones, wastes, and proteins. --- #### **Model Answer to (b):** **The Three Major Plasma Proteins:** | Protein | Normal Range | % of Total Protein | Key Functions | |---|---|---|---| | **Albumin** | **3.5 – 5.0 g/dL** | **~60%** | Generates oncotic pressure (~70–80% of total); transports hormones, fatty acids, drugs, bilirubin | | **Globulins (α, β, γ)** | **2.0 – 3.5 g/dL** | **~36%** | α, β — transport of lipids, metal-binding proteins; γ — **immunoglobulins (antibodies)** | | **Fibrinogen** | **0.2 – 0.4 g/dL** | **~4%** | Final substrate of the clotting cascade; converted to **fibrin** by thrombin | **Albumin and Colloid Osmotic Pressure:** Plasma proteins (especially albumin) are too large to cross the capillary endothelium freely, while water and small ions can. This creates an **oncotic pressure gradient** that pulls fluid back into the capillary at the venous end. $$\pi \approx 25 \; \text{mmHg (at plasma albumin 4 g/dL)}$$ Of this ~25 mmHg, **albumin alone contributes about 70–80%** (~18–20 mmHg); globulins contribute the remainder. **Clinical Significance:** 1. **Hypoalbuminemia** (e.g., liver cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, protein malnutrition) → ↓ oncotic pressure → **edema and ascites** (fluid leaks into interstitial space). 2. **Albumin loss in urine (microalbuminuria)** is an early marker of diabetic nephropathy. 3. **Albumin binds drugs** — hypoalbuminemia alters free-drug levels and pharmacokinetics (e.g., warfarin, phenytoin). --- ### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Choice B]** **(a)** Define hematocrit. With the help of a labeled diagram of a centrifuged blood sample, explain how hematocrit is measured. Discuss the factors affecting hematocrit value. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 70 kg male patient has the following lab values: Hb = 14 g/dL, RBC count = 5 × 10⁶/μL, Hct = 45%. Calculate MCV, MCH, and MCHC. Comment on whether the values are within normal range. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer to (a):** **Definition:** Hematocrit (Hct) is the volume fraction of **packed red blood cells** in whole blood, expressed as a percentage. $$Hct = \frac{V_{RBC}}{V_{whole\,blood}} \times 100\%$$ **Measurement Procedure (Microhematocrit method):** 1. Fill a capillary tube with anticoagulated whole blood. 2. Seal one end with clay. 3. Centrifuge at ~10,000–12,000 g for **5 minutes**. 4. After centrifugation, three layers appear (refer Section 4.2): - **Top — Plasma** (~55%, straw-yellow). - **Middle — Buffy coat** (<1%, WBCs + platelets). - **Bottom — Packed RBCs** (~45%, dark red). 5. Measure the total length of the blood column and the length of the packed RBC column: $$Hct = \frac{\text{Length of RBC column}}{\text{Total length of blood column}} \times 100\%$$ **Factors Affecting Hematocrit:** | Factor | Effect on Hct | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **High altitude** | ↑ Hct | ↑ EPO → ↑ RBC production (compensatory) | | **Dehydration** | ↑ Hct | ↓ plasma volume (hemoconcentration) | | **Overhydration** | ↓ Hct | ↑ plasma volume (hemodilution) | | **Anemia (iron, B12 deficiency)** | ↓ Hct | ↓ RBC production / ↑ RBC loss | | **Polycythemia vera** | ↑ Hct | Myeloproliferative ↑ RBC production | | **Chronic hypoxia (COPD)** | ↑ Hct | ↑ EPO from kidneys | | **Pregnancy** | ↓ Hct | ↑ plasma volume > ↑ RBC mass | | **Smoking** | ↑ Hct | CO-induced chronic hypoxia → ↑ EPO | --- #### **Model Answer to (b):** **Given:** - Hb = 14 g/dL - RBC = 5 × 10⁶/μL - Hct = 45% (= 0.45) **Calculations:** **(i) MCV:** $$MCV = \frac{Hct\,[\%] \times 10}{RBC\,[\times 10^6/\mu L]} = \frac{45 \times 10}{5} = \frac{450}{5} = 90 \; \text{fL}$$ **(ii) MCH:** $$MCH = \frac{Hb\,[g/dL] \times 10}{RBC\,[\times 10^6/\mu L]} = \frac{14 \times 10}{5} = \frac{140}{5} = 28 \; \text{pg}$$ **(iii) MCHC:** $$MCHC = \frac{Hb\,[g/dL]}{Hct\,[\%]} = \frac{14}{45} = 0.311 \; \text{g/dL} = 31.1 \; \text{g/dL}$$ **Comparison with Reference Range:** | Index | Calculated | Reference Range | Status | |---|---|---|---| | MCV | 90 fL | 80 – 100 fL | **Normal (normocytic)** | | MCH | 28 pg | 27 – 33 pg | **Normal (normochromic)** | | MCHC | 31.1 g/dL | 32 – 36 g/dL | **Slightly low (borderline hypochromic)** | **Clinical interpretation:** - The patient is **normocytic-normochromic** with all values essentially within the normal range, suggesting healthy erythropoiesis. - The MCHC is at the lower borderline; if this were symptomatic (fatigue, pallor), iron studies (serum ferritin, TIBC) would be warranted. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing hematocrit with total cellular fraction.** Hematocrit measures *only* RBC volume, not WBCs + platelets. Many texts round "Hct ≈ 45%" to mean "formed elements ≈ 45%", but technically WBCs + platelets add another ~1%. > 2. **Forgetting units in MCV / MCH / MCHC calculations.** Units (fL, pg, g/dL) carry **½ mark each** in KTU valuation. Always write the unit explicitly. > 3. **Mixing up plasma and serum.** Serum = plasma **minus fibrinogen** (and other consumed clotting factors). This is a classic 3-mark trap. > 4. **Stating only the percentage and not the absolute volumes.** When asked for blood composition, give both **% and absolute volume (mL)** for full marks. > 5. **Missing the differential WBC count.** Total WBC count alone is incomplete — you must state the **percentage** of each type (Neutrophils 50–70%, etc.). > 6. **Skipping the role of albumin in oncotic pressure.** This is a "high-weightage" application question frequently asked for 3–5 marks. > 7. **Calculation slips in MCHC.** Use $MCHC = Hb / Hct$ (not $Hb / RBC$). Many students mis-apply the formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Blood = Cells in Plasma"** ### Composition at a Glance - ☐ **Whole blood** = **~55% Plasma** + **~45% Formed Elements** (mostly RBCs). - ☐ **Plasma** = **~91–92% Water + 7–8% Proteins + 1–2% Other Solutes**. - ☐ **Formed Elements** = **~99% RBCs + <1% WBCs + <1% Platelets**. ### The Three Plasma Proteins — Memorize! - ☐ **Albumin** (~60%, ~4 g/dL) → oncotic pressure + transport. - ☐ **Globulins** (~36%, ~2.5 g/dL) → antibodies (γ) + transport (α, β). - ☐ **Fibrinogen** (~4%, ~0.3 g/dL) → clotting. ### Formed Element Counts (MUST memorize) - ☐ **RBC:** ~5 × 10⁶/μL (males), ~4.5 × 10⁶/μL (females). - ☐ **WBC total:** 4,000 – 11,000/μL. - ☐ **Platelets:** 150,000 – 400,000/μL. ### Hematocrit & RBC Indices - ☐ **Hct:** ~45% (males), ~41% (females). - ☐ **MCV** = (Hct × 10)/RBC → 80–100 fL. - ☐ **MCH** = (Hb × 10)/RBC → 27–33 pg. - ☐ **MCHC** = Hb/Hct → 32–36 g/dL. ### Key Distinctions - ☐ **Plasma vs. Serum:** Serum lacks fibrinogen. - ☐ **Buffy coat:** thin middle layer = WBCs + Platelets. - ☐ **Erythrocytes:** **anuclear, biconcave, lifespan 120 days**, contain Hb. - ☐ **Platelets:** **not true cells** — cytoplasmic fragments of megakaryocytes. ### Engineering / Quantitative Hooks - ☐ Blood is a **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning fluid**. - ☐ Viscosity $\eta_{rel} \approx 1 + 2.5 \cdot Hct$ (Einstein relation). - ☐ Total blood volume $\approx 70$ mL/kg body weight. - ☐ Plasma volume $= V_{blood} \cdot (1 - Hct)$. ### Common Viva / Oral Questions - ☐ "Why are RBCs biconcave?" → ↑ SA/V, deformability, gas exchange. - ☐ "Why no nucleus in mature RBC?" → Maximize Hb packing; improve capillary transit. - ☐ "What happens if albumin falls?" → ↓ oncotic pressure → edema (e.g., kwashiorkor, cirrhosis). - ☐ "Why is Hct higher at high altitude?" → Hypoxia → ↑ EPO → ↑ RBC production. > **End of Module 1 Notes — "Blood Consists of Cells Suspended in Plasma"**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hemostasis Defends the Integrity of the Vascular Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Hemostasis** is the tightly regulated, multistep physiological process by which the body preserves the integrity and continuity of the closed, high-pressure **vascular volume** following injury to a blood vessel. It is defined as the arrest of bleeding (Greek: *haima* = blood, *stasis* = halt) through a coordinated triad of **vascular spasm**, **platelet plug formation**, and **blood coagulation (fibrin mesh formation)**, followed by **fibrinolysis** and **vascular repair**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> Hemostasis is the *defense mechanism* of the closed circulatory system. It defends the **vascular volume (≈ 5 L in an adult)** against loss by sealing breaches in vessel walls, maintaining perfusion pressure, and thereby preserving mean arterial pressure (MAP) and tissue oxygen delivery.

The body defends its vascular volume through **three sequential, overlapping phases**:

1. **Primary Hemostasis** (Platelet plug formation, 1–3 min)
2. **Secondary Hemostasis** (Fibrin clot formation via coagulation cascade, 3–6 min)
3. **Tertiary Hemostasis / Fibrinolysis** (Clot dissolution, hours to days)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the cardiovascular system as a **high-pressure municipal water pipeline network** carrying ~5 liters of fluid.

- When a pipe (blood vessel) develops a crack (injury), the system would rapidly lose pressure and volume — leading to **hypovolemic shock** — unless something seals the leak.
- **Hemostasis is the emergency repair crew** dispatched to the leak site:
  - The **plumber's first move** = squeeze the pipe (vascular spasm).
  - The **second move** = drop in temporary rubber plugs (platelet plug).
  - The **third move** = pour quick-setting epoxy (fibrin clot).
  - **After repair** = dissolve the epoxy when no longer needed (fibrinolysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Insight:** Loss of even **10–15 % (~500–750 mL)** of total blood volume can trigger compensatory sympathetic responses; loss of **30–40 %** is life-threatening. Hemostasis exists precisely to keep the system **sealed under these quantitative constraints**.

### 1.3 Quantitative Reference Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Total Blood Volume (Adult) | **≈ 5 L (70 mL/kg)** |
| Circulating Platelet Count | **150,000 – 400,000 /µL** |
| Clotting Time (Lee-White) | **5 – 15 min** |
| Bleeding Time | **2 – 7 min** |
| Prothrombin Time (PT) | **11 – 15 s** |
| Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) | **25 – 40 s** |
| Plasma Fibrinogen | **200 – 400 mg/dL** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of Hemostasis

#### **Phase 1 — Vascular Spasm (Vasoconstriction)**

- **Trigger:** Direct mechanical injury + release of endothelin-1 (ET-1) + thromboxane A₂ (TXA₂) from activated platelets + sympathetic reflex.
- **Mechanism:** Smooth muscle contraction of the arteriole / venule wall → reduced vessel diameter → reduced blood flow → minimizes blood loss.
- **Quantitative Impact (Poiseuille's Law):**

$$Q = \frac{\Delta P \cdot \pi \cdot r^{4}}{8 \eta L}$$

- Because flow $Q \propto r^{4}$, even a **50 % reduction in radius** reduces flow to **(0.5)⁴ = 6.25 %** of original — a **94 % reduction in blood loss**.

#### **Phase 2 — Platelet Plug Formation (Primary Hemostasis)**

- **Steps:**
  1. **Adhesion** – Platelet glycoprotein Ib–V–IX binds **von Willebrand Factor (vWF)** exposed on subendothelial collagen.
  2. **Activation** – Platelets release ADP, TXA₂, serotonin, Ca²⁺.
  3. **Aggregation** – GPIIb/IIIa receptors bind fibrinogen → platelets crosslink → **primary hemostatic plug**.

#### **Phase 3 — Coagulation (Secondary Hemostasis / Fibrin Clot)**

- **Intrinsic Pathway** (contact activation, measured by aPTT): Factor XII → XI → IX → VIII
- **Extrinsic Pathway** (tissue factor, measured by PT): Factor VII + Tissue Factor
- **Common Pathway**: X → V → Prothrombin (II) → Thrombin (IIa) → Fibrinogen (I) → Fibrin (Ia) → XIIIa crosslinks → **Stable fibrin mesh**

#### **Phase 4 — Fibrinolysis (Clot Resolution)**

- **Plasminogen → Plasmin** (catalyzed by tPA).
- Plasmin cleaves fibrin into **D-dimers** and fibrin degradation products (FDPs).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Poiseuille's Law | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P \, \pi \, r^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$ | $\Delta P$ in Pa, $r,L$ in m, $\eta$ in Pa·s |
| Flow reduction by vasoconstriction | $\dfrac{Q_{\text{new}}}{Q_{\text{old}}} = \left(\dfrac{r_{\text{new}}}{r_{\text{old}}}\right)^{4}$ | The **4th-power rule** — KTU favorite |
| Resistance to flow | $R = \dfrac{8 \eta L}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Increases 16× if radius halves |
| Total blood volume | $V_{\text{blood}} = 0.07 \times m_{\text{body}}$ | mL if mass in kg |
| % Volume loss classes | Class I: <15 %; II: 15–30 %; III: 30–40 %; IV: >40 % | ATLS shock classification |
| Mean arterial pressure | $\text{MAP} = \text{DBP} + \dfrac{1}{3}(\text{SBP} - \text{DBP})$ | mmHg |
| Fibrinogen stoichiometry | 1 Fibrinogen → 1 Fibrin monomer + 2 Fibrinopeptide A & B | Thrombin-catalyzed |
| Clot strength (qualitative) | $\propto [\text{Fibrinogen}] \times [\text{Factor XIIIa activity}]$ | mg/dL |
| Antithrombin III efficiency | $v_{\text{inhibited}} \propto [\text{AT-III}] \times [\text{Heparin}]$ | Heparin accelerates 1000× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Poiseuille's law analogy underpins design of **extracorporeal circuits (hemodialysis, ECMO, cardiopulmonary bypass)**. KTU emphasizes quantitative understanding of vessel radius, viscosity, and pressure drop — the same physics govern blood flow and biomedical device flow.

### 2.3 Why Hemostasis Defends Vascular Volume — The Quantitative Logic

1. Without hemostasis, a 2-mm arteriolar breach would lose **~250 mL/min** (exsanguination in <20 min).
2. Vasoconstriction reduces flow to **~15 mL/min**.
3. Platelet plug + fibrin clot reduces residual leak to **near zero (≤1 mL/min)**.
4. Total blood volume **5 L** is therefore preserved → **MAP maintained** → tissue perfusion sustained.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Derivation — Effect of Vascular Spasm on Blood Loss

We will quantitatively prove why vasoconstriction is the *first and most effective* line of volume defense.

**Given:**
- Initial vessel radius: $r_1$
- Constricted vessel radius: $r_2 = 0.5 \, r_1$ (50 % constriction)
- Viscosity $\eta$ and length $L$ remain constant.

**Step 1 — Apply Poiseuille's Law initially:**

$$Q_1 = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi \, r_1^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Poiseuille's Law after constriction:**

$$Q_2 = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi \, r_2^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio to eliminate common terms:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{r_2^{4}}{r_1^{4}} = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r_2 = 0.5 \, r_1$:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\frac{0.5 \, r_1}{r_1}\right)^{4} = (0.5)^{4} = 0.0625$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$Q_2 = 0.0625 \, Q_1 = 6.25\% \text{ of } Q_1$$

Therefore, blood loss is reduced by:

$$\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.0625) \times 100\% = 93.75\%$$

> This single derivation explains why vasospasm alone can reduce blood loss by **nearly 94 %** — a powerful quantitative defense of vascular volume.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step — Mass Balance of Vascular Volume During Hemorrhage

Let $V_0$ = initial blood volume (5 L), $V_L$ = volume lost, $V_T$ = total compensation (fluid shift + transfusion).

**Step 1 — Net volume after uncontrolled loss:**

$$V_{\text{net}} = V_0 - V_L$$

**Step 2 — For a Class II hemorrhage ($V_L = 20\% \times V_0$):**

$$V_L = 0.20 \times 5 = 1.0 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Residual vascular volume:**

$$V_{\text{net}} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4 — MAP compensation (assuming SVR rises via sympathetic reflex):**

$$\text{MAP}_2 = \text{MAP}_1 \times \frac{V_{\text{net}}}{V_0} \times \frac{\text{SVR}_2}{\text{SVR}_1}$$

**Step 5 — For a typical 20 % loss with 2× SVR rise:**

$$\text{MAP}_2 = 93 \times \frac{4}{5} \times 2 = 148.8 \text{ mmHg (transient)}$$

> This illustrates the body's *quantitative defense* of vascular volume via reflex vasoconstriction (the very mechanism hemostasis amplifies locally).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hemostasis Pathway Summary

| Phase | Trigger | Key Cells / Factors | Time | Output | Defends Volume by |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vascular Spasm | Injury, ET-1, TXA₂ | Endothelium, smooth muscle | Seconds | Vessel narrowing | Reducing flow (Q ∝ r⁴) |
| Platelet Plug | Collagen, vWF | Platelets (150–400k/µL) | 1–3 min | Temporary plug | Mechanical seal |
| Coagulation | TF, Contact | Factors II, VII, IX, X, V, VIII, XIII | 3–6 min | Fibrin mesh | Permanent seal |
| Fibrinolysis | tPA | Plasmin | Hours–days | Clot lysis | Restores flow |
| Repair | PDGF, VEGF | Endothelial cells, fibroblasts | Days–weeks | New vessel wall | Permanent integrity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hemostasis Cascade — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vascular Injury] --> B[Phase 1: Vascular Spasm]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Platelet Plug Formation]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Coagulation Cascade]
    D --> E[Stable Fibrin Clot]
    E --> F[Phase 4: Fibrinolysis]
    F --> G[Clot Lysis and Vessel Repair]
    G --> H[Restored Vascular Volume Integrity]

    B --> B1[Endothelin-1 Release]
    B --> B2[Thromboxane A2 Release]
    B --> B3[Sympathetic Reflex]
    B1 --> B
    B2 --> B
    B3 --> B

    C --> C1[Platelet Adhesion via vWF]
    C --> C2[Platelet Activation ADP TXA2]
    C --> C3[Platelet Aggregation via Fibrinogen]
    C1 --> C
    C2 --> C
    C3 --> C

    D --> D1[Extrinsic Pathway Factor VII TF]
    D --> D2[Intrinsic Pathway Factors XII XI IX VIII]
    D --> D3[Common Pathway Factor X V Thrombin Fibrinogen]
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3

    F --> F1[tPA Release]
    F --> F2[Plasminogen to Plasmin]
    F --> F3[Fibrin Cleavage to D-dimers]
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
```

### 4.2 Coagulation Cascade — Detailed Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[Extrinsic Pathway] -->|Factor VII + TF| COM[Common Pathway]
    INT[Intrinsic Pathway] -->|Factors XII XI IX VIII| COM
    COM -->|Factor X + V| PT[Prothrombin to Thrombin]
    PT -->|Thrombin| FB[Fibrinogen to Fibrin Monomer]
    FB -->|Factor XIIIa| CL[Crosslinked Fibrin Clot]
    CL -->|tPA| PL[Plasminogen to Plasmin]
    PL -->|Cleavage| DD[D-dimers and FDPs]
```

### 4.3 Vascular Volume Defense — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0[Initial Blood Volume 5 L] --> V1[Injury Event]
    V1 --> V2[Uncontrolled Loss Risk]
    V2 --> V3[Hemostasis Activation]
    V3 --> V4[Vasospasm Reduces Q by 94%]
    V4 --> V5[Platelet Plug Seals Breach]
    V5 --> V6[Fibrin Clot Reinforces Seal]
    V6 --> V7[Volume Preserved Approx 5 L]
    V7 --> V8[MAP Maintained Approx 93 mmHg]
    V8 --> V9[Tissue Perfusion Sustained]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define hemostasis and list its three classical phases.*

**Model Answer:**
Hemostasis (Greek: *haima* = blood, *stasis* = halt) is the physiological process that arrests bleeding and preserves the integrity of the vascular volume following vessel injury. The three classical phases are:
1. **Vascular spasm** (vasoconstriction)
2. **Platelet plug formation** (primary hemostasis)
3. **Blood coagulation / fibrin clot formation** (secondary hemostasis)
A fourth phase, **fibrinolysis**, restores vessel patency after repair.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Poiseuille's law and explain its relevance to vascular volume defense during hemostasis.*

**Model Answer:**
Poiseuille's law states:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi \, r^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

where $Q$ is flow, $\Delta P$ is pressure gradient, $r$ is vessel radius, $\eta$ is viscosity, and $L$ is vessel length.
**Relevance:** Because flow $Q \propto r^4$, vasoconstriction during hemostasis dramatically reduces blood loss — a 50 % reduction in radius reduces flow to **6.25 %** of original, defending vascular volume.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the three phases of hemostasis with their cellular and molecular mediators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Poiseuille's law, derive the quantitative reduction in blood flow when vessel radius is reduced by 50 % during vasospasm. Discuss its significance in defending vascular volume. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Vascular Spasm (Phase 1):** Reflex contraction of vascular smooth muscle triggered by (i) direct myogenic injury, (ii) release of **endothelin-1 (ET-1)** and **thromboxane A₂ (TXA₂)** from endothelium/platelets, and (iii) sympathetic reflex activation. Effect: vessel constriction, reduced flow.
2. **Platelet Plug Formation (Phase 2):**
   - *Adhesion:* Glycoprotein Ib–V–IX receptors on platelets bind **von Willebrand Factor (vWF)** anchored to subendothelial collagen.
   - *Activation:* Platelets release **ADP, TXA₂, serotonin, Ca²⁺**, and synthesize thromboxane A₂.
   - *Aggregation:* GPIIb/IIIa receptors crosslink platelets via **fibrinogen**, forming a temporary plug (primary hemostasis).
3. **Coagulation (Phase 3):**
   - *Extrinsic pathway:* Tissue Factor (TF) + Factor VIIa activates Factor X.
   - *Intrinsic pathway:* Contact activation cascade (XII → XI → IX → VIII) activates Factor X.
   - *Common pathway:* Factor Xa + Va converts **Prothrombin (II) → Thrombin (IIa)**, which converts **Fibrinogen (I) → Fibrin monomers**, which are crosslinked by **Factor XIIIa** into a stable mesh.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Write Poiseuille's law:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi \, r^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

**Step 2:** Initial flow:

$$Q_1 = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi \, r_1^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

**Step 3:** After 50 % constriction, $r_2 = 0.5 \, r_1$:

$$Q_2 = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi \, (0.5 r_1)^{4}}{8 \, \eta \, L}$$

**Step 4:** Take ratio:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = (0.5)^{4} = 0.0625$$

**Step 5:** Percentage reduction:

$$\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.0625) \times 100\% = 93.75\%$$

**Step 6 — Significance:** A mere 50 % vasoconstriction reduces blood loss by **~94 %**, which is the principal quantitative mechanism by which hemostasis defends the 5 L vascular volume.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the coagulation cascade with a neat flowchart. Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic pathways. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient suffers a Class II hemorrhage losing 20 % of 5 L blood volume. Calculate the residual vascular volume and the percentage of blood volume defended by hemostasis if vasospasm reduces flow by 90 %. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **coagulation cascade** is a series of enzymatic activations of clotting factors (numbered I–XIII) culminating in fibrin formation.

- **Extrinsic Pathway** (fast, seconds): Tissue Factor (TF, Factor III) exposed on subendothelial cells binds **Factor VIIa** → activates **Factor X**. Measured by **Prothrombin Time (PT)**.
- **Intrinsic Pathway** (slower, minutes): Contact with negatively charged surfaces activates **Factor XII → XI → IX**, with **Factor VIIIa** as cofactor → activates **Factor X**. Measured by **aPTT**.
- **Common Pathway:** Factor Xa + Va + Ca²⁺ + phospholipid → **prothrombinase complex** → converts Prothrombin (II) to **Thrombin (IIa)** → cleaves Fibrinogen (I) into **Fibrin monomers** → crosslinked by **Factor XIIIa**.

(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 for the schematic.)

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Calculate blood loss:

$$V_L = 0.20 \times 5 \text{ L} = 1.0 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2:** Residual vascular volume:

$$V_{\text{residual}} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ L} \quad (80\% \text{ of original})$$

**Step 3:** Effective loss if vasospasm reduces flow by 90 %:

$$V_{\text{effective loss}} = 1.0 \times (1 - 0.90) = 0.1 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4:** Effective volume defended:

$$V_{\text{defended}} = 5 - 0.1 = 4.9 \text{ L} \quad (98\%)$$

**Step 5:** Percentage defended:

$$\frac{4.9}{5} \times 100\% = 98\%$$

**Conclusion:** Hemostasis defends **98 %** of vascular volume even in a Class II hemorrhage scenario, validating the quantitative importance of the hemostatic response.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4th-power rule:** Many students write "vasoconstriction reduces flow by 50 %" when radius halves — the correct answer is **93.75 %**, not 50 %. This is a **3-mark penalty hotspot**.
> 2. **Confusing intrinsic vs extrinsic pathways:** Extrinsic = TF + VII (PT test). Intrinsic = contact factors XII, XI, IX, VIII (aPTT test). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping Fibrinogen → Fibrin stoichiometry:** Always state thrombin cleaves fibrinopeptide A and B from fibrinogen to form fibrin monomers, which Factor XIIIa crosslinks.
> 4. **Omitting the role of Ca²⁺:** Calcium (Factor IV) is mandatory in multiple steps; examiners frequently deduct for missing it.
> 5. **Writing "blood clotting = hemostasis":** Hemostasis is the *entire* process including vasospasm, platelet plug, coagulation, AND fibrinolysis — not just clotting.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hemostasis = defense of vascular volume** (5 L in adults, 70 mL/kg).
- **Three phases:** Vascular spasm → Platelet plug → Fibrin clot (coagulation).
- **Vasospasm's quantitative power:** $Q \propto r^4$ → 50 % radius reduction = **93.75 %** flow reduction.
- **Platelet adhesion** requires **vWF**; aggregation requires **fibrinogen** binding GPIIb/IIIa.
- **Extrinsic pathway** = TF + Factor VII → measured by **PT** (normal: 11–15 s).
- **Intrinsic pathway** = XII, XI, IX, VIII → measured by **aPTT** (normal: 25–40 s).
- **Common pathway** = X, V, II (thrombin), I (fibrinogen), XIII (crosslinking).
- **Ca²⁺ (Factor IV)** is essential in both intrinsic and common pathways.
- **Fibrinolysis:** tPA converts plasminogen → plasmin → cleaves fibrin into **D-dimers**.
- **Anticoagulant safeguards:** Antithrombin III, Protein C/S, TFPI — prevent runaway clotting.
- **Clinical correlates:** Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency), Hemophilia B (Factor IX), von Willebrand disease (vWF defect), DIC (consumption coagulopathy).
- **Engineering parallel:** Hemostasis principles underpin design of **biocompatible vascular grafts, ECMO circuits, and stents** — all KTU-relevant biomedical engineering applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma and Red Blood Cells: Plasma Proteins and Electrolytes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT415 - QUANTITATIVE PHYSIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plasma and Red Blood Cells: Plasma Proteins and Electrolytes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Blood Plasma
**Plasma** is the straw-colored, acellular, aqueous extracellular fluid component of blood, obtained by centrifuging whole blood (with anticoagulant) to sediment the cellular elements (RBCs, WBCs, Platelets). It constitutes approximately **55% of total blood volume** in healthy adults. Plasma is fundamentally a colloidal solution of proteins suspended in an electrolyte-rich aqueous medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serum vs Plasma — KTU Examiner's Hot Trap**
> - **Plasma** = Whole blood minus the cells (anticoagulant added). Contains **FIBRINOGEN**.
> - **Serum** = Whole blood minus cells AND clotting factors (no anticoagulant, blood is allowed to clot). **NO FIBRINOGEN** in serum.
> - This distinction carries **frequently asked 3-mark weightage** in KTU board exams.

### 1.2 Definition of Red Blood Cells (Erythrocytes)
**Red Blood Cells (RBCs)** are biconcave, anucleate, hemoglobin-filled cells derived from hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow. Their principal physiological role is oxygen (O₂) transport from the lungs to peripheral tissues and partial transport of carbon dioxide (CO₂) back to the lungs. A healthy adult male has **4.5–5.5 million RBCs/μL** of blood; females have **4.0–4.5 million RBCs/μL**.

### 1.3 Definition of Plasma Proteins
Plasma proteins are high-molecular-weight, biologically synthesized macromolecules (predominantly by the **liver**) that remain in circulation and perform diverse transport, osmotic, immunological, and hemostatic functions. Total plasma protein concentration in healthy humans is **6.0–8.0 g/dL**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Plasma Protein Fractions (separated via electrophoresis):**
> 1. **Albumin** (~60% of total plasma protein; 3.5–5.0 g/dL)
> 2. **Globulins** (~36%; subdivided into α1, α2, β, and γ globulins; 2.0–3.0 g/dL)
> 3. **Fibrinogen** (~4%; 0.2–0.4 g/dL)

### 1.4 Definition of Plasma Electrolytes
**Plasma electrolytes** are inorganic ions dissolved in plasma water that establish osmotic gradients, generate transmembrane electrical potentials, buffer pH, and catalyze enzymatic reactions. Total plasma electrolyte concentration is governed by **ionic equilibrium (Donnan distribution)** due to impermeable plasma proteins.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cargo Ship" Model

Imagine **plasma as the Mediterranean Sea** and **RBCs as cargo ships** sailing through it:

- The **sea (plasma)** carries dissolved **salt (electrolytes)** — this allows the sea to retain its volume and keeps the ships afloat.
- The **cargo ships (RBCs)** carry valuable hemoglobin — their inside content must be balanced with the sea's salt concentration, or they will either swell and burst (if the sea is too dilute) or shrink (if the sea is too salty).
- Floating in the sea are **large sponge-like rafts (plasma proteins)** that absorb water, act as cargo trucks for hormones/fat, and resist water escaping the bloodstream.
- Some ships have onboard doors that pump ions in and out (active transport via the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** in RBC membranes).

This mental model captures the central theme: **plasma proteins and electrolytes are interdependent regulators of volume, pH, and electrical neutrality** in the blood.

### 1.6 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Reference Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Plasma volume fraction | **55** | % of total blood |
| Total plasma protein | **6.0–8.0** | g/dL |
| Plasma albumin | **3.5–5.0** | g/dL |
| Plasma globulin | **2.0–3.0** | g/dL |
| Plasma fibrinogen | **0.2–0.4** | g/dL |
| A/G ratio | **1.2–1.5** | dimensionless |
| Plasma osmolality | **285–295** | mOsm/kg H₂O |
| Plasma oncotic pressure (π) | **25–28** | mmHg |
| Arterial plasma pH | **7.35–7.45** | dimensionless |
| RBC count (Male) | **4.5–5.5** | × 10⁶/μL |
| RBC count (Female) | **4.0–4.5** | × 10⁶/μL |
| Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) | **80–100** | fL |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Plasma Proteins — The Functional Trilogy

Plasma proteins are categorized by their electrophoretic mobility and biological function. The KTU syllabus expects you to map **each protein fraction to its dominant physiological role**.

**A) Albumin (M.W. ≈ 69,000 Da; 3.5–5.0 g/dL)**
- Synthesized exclusively by the liver at a rate of ~12 g/day.
- Most abundant plasma protein — responsible for ~**75–80% of plasma oncotic pressure**.
- Acts as a **universal carrier** for free fatty acids, bilirubin, steroid hormones, thyroxine, and pharmacological drugs (e.g., warfarin, aspirin, sulfonamides).
- Negative charge at physiological pH (~pI = 4.7) — forms the basis of the **Donnan distribution** of plasma electrolytes.

**B) Globulins (α1, α2, β, γ; 2.0–3.0 g/dL)**
- **α1-globulins**: α1-antitrypsin (protease inhibitor), thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG), transcortin.
- **α2-globulins**: Ceruloplasmin (Cu²⁺ transport), haptoglobin (binds free hemoglobin), α2-macroglobulin.
- **β-globulins**: Transferrin (Fe³⁺ transport), complement components (C3, C4), low-density lipoproteins (LDL).
- **γ-globulins = Immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgD, IgE)** — antibodies produced by plasma cells.

**C) Fibrinogen (M.W. ≈ 340,000 Da; 0.2–0.4 g/dL)**
- Soluble precursor of fibrin; synthesized in the liver.
- Essential for **blood clotting (hemostasis)**.
- Major contributor to **erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)** because it neutralizes RBC surface charges and promotes rouleaux formation.

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Pearl:** A low A/G ratio (<1.0) suggests **liver dysfunction (cirrhosis)** because albumin synthesis falls while IgG may rise. An inversion >1.0 is a board-favorite viva question.

### 2.2 Classification of Plasma Electrolytes — Cations vs Anions

Plasma water contains ions in milliequivalent (mEq/L) concentrations. **Electrical neutrality** must always hold: total cations = total anions = ~**155 mEq/L**.

**Major Cations:**
- **Na⁺** (Sodium): **135–145 mEq/L** — dominant extracellular cation. Governs plasma osmolality and ECF volume.
- **K⁺** (Potassium): **3.5–5.0 mEq/L** — dominant intracellular cation; critical for resting membrane potential and cardiac repolarization.
- **Ca²⁺** (Ionized Calcium): **4.5–5.5 mg/dL** (total) or **2.2–2.6 mEq/L** (ionized) — bone mineralization, neuromuscular excitability, second messenger.
- **Mg²⁺** (Ionized): **1.5–2.0 mEq/L** — cofactor for >300 enzymatic reactions.

**Major Anions:**
- **Cl⁻** (Chloride): **98–107 mEq/L** — dominant extracellular anion; principal partner of Na⁺ for electroneutrality.
- **HCO₃⁻** (Bicarbonate): **22–28 mEq/L** — central component of the **acid-base buffer system**.
- **HPO₄²⁻/H₂PO₄⁻** (Phosphate): **0.8–1.5 mEq/L** — intracellular anion; pH buffering inside cells.
- **SO₄²⁻** (Sulfate): trace amounts.
- **Organic acids**: Lactate, citrate, pyruvate.

### 2.3 Quantitative Physiology — The Starling & Donnan Frameworks

**A) Starling's Equation (Microvascular Fluid Exchange):**
The net fluid movement across the capillary membrane depends on four pressures (Starling forces):
- Capillary hydrostatic pressure (Pc)
- Interstitial hydrostatic pressure (Pi)
- Plasma oncotic pressure (πp) — *driven by plasma proteins*
- Interstitial oncotic pressure (πi)

**B) Donnan Effect (Gibbs–Donnan Equilibrium):**
Plasma proteins are large, negatively charged, and **impermeable** to the capillary membrane. This creates an asymmetric distribution of small diffusible ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻) across the membrane. The result:
- The plasma side has **slightly higher cation concentration** than the interstitial side.
- The plasma side has **slightly lower anion concentration** than the interstitial side.
- This asymmetry contributes ~**1 mOsm/L** to plasma osmolality and is essential for **Donnan potential** across membranes.

**C) Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation:**
Buffers maintain plasma pH at 7.40. The principal plasma buffer pair is **bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)** and **carbonic acid (H₂CO₃)**.

**D) Osmolality Calculations:**
Plasma osmolality is primarily determined by Na⁺ and its associated anions, with small contributions from glucose and urea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are RBCs used as a "Clinical Mirror" for plasma composition?**
> RBCs are simple, abundant cells whose membrane potential, intracellular ionic composition, and hemoglobin-oxygen affinity directly reflect plasma electrolyte and protein status. Clinically, **Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC)**, **MCV**, and **MCH** are derived from RBC indices and used as diagnostic markers.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim — it covers **90% of numerical problems** in KTU board exams on this topic.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definition | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Van 't Hoff (Oncotic) Pressure | $\pi = c \cdot R \cdot T$ | $c$ = solute concentration; $R$ = gas constant (**0.0821 L·atm/mol·K**); $T$ = absolute temperature | atm or mmHg |
| Osmotic Pressure (in mmHg) | $\pi = n \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T / V$ | $n$ = osmotic coefficient; $C$ = molarity | mmHg |
| Plasma Osmolality (Simplified) | $\text{Osm} = 2 \cdot [\text{Na}^{+}] + \dfrac{[\text{Glucose}]}{18} + \dfrac{[\text{BUN}]}{2.8}$ | BUN = Blood Urea Nitrogen | mOsm/kg |
| Osmolar Gap | $\text{Gap} = \text{Measured Osm} - \text{Calculated Osm}$ | Detects unmeasured osmoles (e.g., ethanol, methanol) | mOsm/kg |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = \text{pK}_a + \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}]}{[\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3}]}$ | $\text{pK}_a = 6.1$; $[\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3}] = 0.03 \cdot \text{PaCO}_{2}$ | dimensionless |
| Modified Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}]}{0.03 \cdot \text{PaCO}_{2}}$ | PaCO₂ = arterial CO₂ partial pressure | dimensionless |
| Nernst Equation (RBC membrane) | $E = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{z \cdot F} \cdot \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$ | $z$ = ionic valence; $F$ = Faraday constant (**96,485 C/mol**) | volts (V) |
| Nernst (base-10 form) | $E = \dfrac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \cdot \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$ | At 37°C | mV |
| Donnan Ratio | $r = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^{+}]_{i}}{[\text{Na}^{+}]_{o}} = \dfrac{[\text{Cl}^{-}]_{o}}{[\text{Cl}^{-}]_{i}}$ | $r$ = Donnan factor (≈ 1.04 in plasma) | dimensionless |
| Albumin/Globulin Ratio | $\text{A/G} = \dfrac{[\text{Albumin}]}{[\text{Globulin}]}$ | Normal = **1.2–1.5** | dimensionless |
| Starling Net Filtration | $J_v = K_f \cdot [(P_c - P_i) - \sigma(\pi_p - \pi_i)]$ | $K_f$ = filtration coefficient; $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient | mL/min |
| MCHC | $\text{MCHC} = \dfrac{\text{Hgb}}{\text{Hct}} \times 100$ | Hgb = hemoglobin; Hct = hematocrit | g/dL |
| MCV | $\text{MCV} = \dfrac{\text{Hct} \times 10}{\text{RBC count}}$ | Standard indices | fL |

### 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

1. **Dialysis Machine Design:** Hemodialysis relies on the **Donnan principle** to selectively remove urea and other nitrogenous wastes while retaining plasma proteins. Bioengineers design dialysis membranes with specific molecular weight cut-offs based on these protein/electrolyte dynamics.
2. **Intravenous (IV) Fluid Formulation:** Ringer's Lactate and Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl) are formulated to mimic plasma osmolality and major electrolyte concentrations — directly derived from the quantitative framework above.
3. **Blood Substitute Engineering:** Perfluorocarbons and hemoglobin-based oxygen carriers (HBOCs) must be designed to match plasma oncotic pressure to avoid circulatory collapse.
4. **Point-of-Care Biosensors:** Ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) used in arterial blood gas (ABG) analyzers quantify Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, and Cl⁻ using the Nernst equation in real time.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Derivation of Plasma Osmolality Using Na⁺ Multiplier

Plasma osmolality is dominated by NaCl, which dissociates into two osmotically active particles (Na⁺ + Cl⁻). For every 1 mEq/L of Na⁺ in plasma, there is approximately 1 mOsm of associated anion.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We start from the definition of osmolality:

$$
\text{Osmolality} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot n_i
$$

where $C_i$ = molar concentration of species $i$, $n_i$ = number of dissociable particles.

For NaCl in plasma: $C_{\text{NaCl}} \cdot n_{\text{NaCl}} = C_{\text{NaCl}} \cdot 2$.

Therefore, the contribution of NaCl to total plasma osmolality is:

$$
\text{Osm}_{\text{NaCl}} = 2 \cdot [\text{Na}^{+}] \quad \text{(mOsm/kg)}
$$

Adding the smaller contributors (glucose and urea):

$$
\text{Osm}_{\text{plasma}} = 2 \cdot [\text{Na}^{+}] + \dfrac{[\text{Glucose}]}{18} + \dfrac{[\text{BUN}]}{2.8}
$$

The denominators **18 and 2.8** convert mg/dL → mmol/L for glucose (M.W. = 180) and urea nitrogen (M.W. = 28) respectively.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $[\text{Na}^{+}] = 140$ mEq/L; $[\text{Glucose}] = 90$ mg/dL; $[\text{BUN}] = 14$ mg/dL.

$$
\text{Osm} = 2 \cdot 140 + \dfrac{90}{18} + \dfrac{14}{2.8}
$$

$$
\text{Osm} = 280 + 5.0 + 5.0 = 290 \text{ mOsm/kg}
$$

This matches the physiological reference range (285–295 mOsm/kg). ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Plasma Oncotic Pressure via Van 't Hoff Equation

The plasma oncotic pressure is the osmotic pressure generated exclusively by the impermeable plasma proteins (colloids). Applying the Van 't Hoff equation for colloidal solutions:

$$
\pi = c \cdot R \cdot T
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

For albumin, which has M.W. ≈ **69,000 g/mol** and plasma concentration ≈ **4.5 g/dL = 45 g/L**:

$$
c_{\text{albumin}} = \dfrac{45 \text{ g/L}}{69{,}000 \text{ g/mol}} = 6.52 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}
$$

Substituting into Van 't Hoff:

$$
\pi_{\text{albumin}} = (6.52 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (0.0821 \text{ L·atm/mol·K}) \cdot (310 \text{ K})
$$

$$
\pi_{\text{albumin}} = 1.66 \times 10^{-2} \text{ atm} = 12.6 \text{ mmHg}
$$

Since albumin contributes ~75–80% of total oncotic pressure:

$$
\pi_{\text{total plasma}} \approx \dfrac{12.6}{0.78} \approx 25.4 \text{ mmHg}
$$

This matches the physiological reference (**25–28 mmHg**). ✓

**Key Insight:** Globulins and fibrinogen, despite being fewer in mass, contribute the remaining ~25% because their larger molecular weights (especially fibrinogen at 340 kDa) generate disproportionately more oncotic pressure per gram.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Donnan Equilibrium Ratio for Plasma Electrolytes

When impermeable anionic proteins ($P^{-}$) are confined to one side of a semipermeable membrane (plasma), the small diffusible cations and anions redistribute to maintain electrochemical equilibrium.

**Setup:** Let plasma contain initial $[P^{-}] = c$. The Donnan equilibrium requires:

$$
[\text{Na}^{+}]_p \cdot [\text{Cl}^{-}]_p = [\text{Na}^{+}]_i \cdot [\text{Cl}^{-}]_i
$$

Let $x$ = moles of NaCl that diffuse from interstitium (i) → plasma (p) at equilibrium.

$$
[\text{Na}^{+}]_p = c + x, \quad [\text{Cl}^{-}]_p = x
$$

$$
[\text{Na}^{+}]_i = c_i - x, \quad [\text{Cl}^{-}]_i = c_i - x
$$

Where $c_i$ = initial interstitial NaCl concentration.

Applying the Donnan product condition:

$$
(c + x)(x) = (c_i - x)^2
$$

Expanding and simplifying (square-rooting both sides for the positive root):

$$
\sqrt{(c+x) \cdot x} = c_i - x
$$

Solving yields the Donnan factor $r$:

$$
r = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^{+}]_p}{[\text{Na}^{+}]_i} = \dfrac{[\text{Cl}^{-}]_i}{[\text{Cl}^{-}]_p}
$$

For normal plasma with $[\text{Protein}^{-}] \approx 1$ mEq/L, $r \approx$ **1.04**.

**Quantitative Consequence:**

If interstitial $[\text{Na}^{+}] = 145$ mEq/L, then plasma $[\text{Na}^{+}] = 145 \times 1.04 \approx 150.8$ mEq/L, while plasma $[\text{Cl}^{-}] = 145 / 1.04 \approx 139.4$ mEq/L.

> [!NOTE]
> This small imbalance (≈4% excess cation) is precisely the mechanism by which plasma proteins generate a measurable **Donnan potential** across capillary walls — a **board-favorite 14-mark derivation question**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Henderson–Hasselbalch for Plasma pH

The carbonic acid/bicarbonate buffer system governs plasma pH. From the equilibrium:

$$
\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \text{HCO}_{3}^{-} + \text{H}^{+}
$$

The acid dissociation constant:

$$
K_a = \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}] \cdot [\text{H}^{+}]}{[\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3}]}
$$

Taking $-\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$
-\log K_a = -\log [\text{H}^{+}] + \log \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}]}{[\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3}]}
$$

By definition, $\text{pK}_a = -\log K_a$ and $\text{pH} = -\log [\text{H}^{+}]$, therefore:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{pK}_a + \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}]}{[\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3}]}
$$

Substituting $\text{pK}_a = 6.1$ and $[\text{H}_{2}\text{CO}_{3}] = 0.03 \cdot \text{PaCO}_{2}$:

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}]}{0.03 \cdot \text{PaCO}_{2}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}] = 24$ mEq/L; $\text{PaCO}_{2} = 40$ mmHg.

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \dfrac{24}{0.03 \cdot 40} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \dfrac{24}{1.2}
$$

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10}(20) = 6.1 + 1.301 = 7.401
$$

This matches the normal arterial plasma pH of **7.40**. ✓

---

### 3.5 Derivation — Nernst Equation for RBC Membrane Potential

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium membrane potential for a single ion. For a typical RBC:

**Given values:** Intracellular $[\text{K}^{+}]_i = 140$ mEq/L; Plasma $[\text{K}^{+}]_o = 4.5$ mEq/L; Temperature = 310 K; $z = +1$.

**Step 1:** Convert concentrations to activities (assume activity coefficient ≈ 1).

**Step 2:** Apply the Nernst equation:

$$
E_{\text{K}^{+}} = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{z \cdot F} \cdot \ln \dfrac{[\text{K}^{+}]_o}{[\text{K}^{+}]_i}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute universal constants:

$$
E_{\text{K}^{+}} = \dfrac{(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(310 \text{ K})}{(1)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})} \cdot \ln \left(\dfrac{4.5}{140}\right)
$$

$$
E_{\text{K}^{+}} = 0.0267 \cdot \ln(0.0321) = 0.0267 \cdot (-3.44) = -91.8 \text{ mV}
$$

This represents the **K⁺ Nernst potential** in RBCs — the equilibrium potential if the membrane were only permeable to K⁺. The actual measured RBC membrane potential is closer to **−10 to −20 mV** because the membrane is also slightly permeable to Na⁺ and Cl⁻.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Calculations

| Quantity | Input Parameters | Calculation | Result | Physiological Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plasma Osmolality | Na⁺=140; Glu=90; BUN=14 | $2(140) + 90/18 + 14/2.8$ | **290 mOsm/kg** | 285–295 ✓ |
| Albumin Oncotic Pressure | c=6.52×10⁻⁴ M; T=310K | $cRT$ | **12.6 mmHg** | ~14 (albumin alone) ✓ |
| Total Plasma π | Albumin contribution ÷ 0.78 | $12.6/0.78$ | **25.4 mmHg** | 25–28 ✓ |
| Donnan Factor (Plasma) | c=1 mEq/L | $r$ ≈ 1.04 | **1.04** | 1.02–1.05 ✓ |
| Plasma pH | HCO₃=24; PaCO₂=40 | $6.1 + \log(24/1.2)$ | **7.401** | 7.35–7.45 ✓ |
| K⁺ Nernst Potential | $[\text{K}^{+}]_i$=140; $[\text{K}^{+}]_o$=4.5 | $(61.5)\log(4.5/140)$ | **−91.8 mV** |  |
| A/G Ratio | Albumin=4.0; Globulin=2.8 | $4.0/2.8$ | **1.43** | 1.2–1.5 ✓ |
| MCHC | Hgb=15; Hct=45 | $(15/45)\cdot100$ | **33.3 g/dL** | 32–36 ✓ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**
**Q: Differentiate between plasma and serum. List the three principal plasma protein fractions with their normal concentrations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plasma | Serum |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Liquid component of blood with anticoagulant | Liquid component after clotting |
| Fibrinogen | **Present** | **Absent** (consumed in clotting) |
| Clotting factors | **All present** | Many consumed/depleted |
| Preparation | Centrifuge anticoagulated blood | Centrifuge clotted blood |
| Use in lab | Coagulation studies | Most chemistry/immunology assays |

**Three Plasma Protein Fractions:**
1. **Albumin** — 3.5–5.0 g/dL (60% of total protein)
2. **Globulins** — 2.0–3.0 g/dL (36%, comprising α1, α2, β, γ)
3. **Fibrinogen** — 0.2–0.4 g/dL (4%)

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Q: State the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation. Calculate the plasma pH given [HCO₃⁻] = 24 mEq/L and PaCO₂ = 40 mmHg.**

**Model Answer:**

The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation for the bicarbonate buffer system is:

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_{3}^{-}]}{0.03 \cdot \text{PaCO}_{2}}
$$

**Substituting values:**

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \dfrac{24}{0.03 \cdot 40} = 6.1 + \log_{10} (20)
$$

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + 1.301 = 7.401
$$

**Result:** Plasma pH = **7.40** (within the normal arterial range of 7.35–7.45).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] Classify plasma proteins in detail. Explain the role of albumin in maintaining plasma oncotic pressure and as a transport protein.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the Donnan equilibrium condition for plasma electrolytes. Calculate the plasma concentration of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ given interstitial concentrations of 145 mEq/L each and Donnan factor r = 1.04.**

---

##### **Part (a) — Model Solution:**

**Classification of Plasma Proteins (Electrophoretic Separation):**

| Fraction | Concentration (g/dL) | Molecular Weight (Da) | Key Functions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Albumin | 3.5–5.0 | ~69,000 | Oncotic pressure, transport |
| α1-globulin | 0.1–0.4 | ~45,000 | Protease inhibition (α1-AT), TBG |
| α2-globulin | 0.4–0.9 | ~60,000 | Haptoglobin, ceruloplasmin |
| β-globulin | 0.5–1.1 | ~90,000 | Transferrin, complement |
| γ-globulin | 0.6–1.6 | ~150,000 | Immunoglobulins (IgG/A/M) |
| Fibrinogen | 0.2–0.4 | ~340,000 | Clotting (fibrin precursor) |

**Role of Albumin in Plasma Oncotic Pressure:**

Albumin generates ~75–80% of total plasma oncotic pressure due to:
1. **High plasma concentration** (3.5–5.0 g/dL — most abundant protein).
2. **Small molecular weight** (69 kDa) — more particles per gram than larger proteins.
3. **Net negative charge** at physiological pH (pI ≈ 4.7), which contributes to the Donnan effect.

By Van 't Hoff: $\pi = cRT$, albumin alone produces **~12.6 mmHg** (out of the total ~25–28 mmHg).

**Role as Transport Protein:**
