Carotid sinus, Aortic arch"] CHEMO["Chemoreceptors
Peripheral and central"] STRETCH["Cardiopulmonary Stretch Receptors"] end SYM --> SA SYM --> AV PAR --> SA PAR --> AV HUM --> SA SA --> ATR AV --> VEN HIS --> VEN VEN --> EJECT BARO -.Modulates.-> SYM BARO -.Modulates.-> PAR CHEMO -.Modulates.-> SYM STRETCH -.Modulates.-> PAR style SA fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff style AV fill:#ffd93d,color:#000 style EJECT fill:#27ae60,color:#fff style BARO fill:#3498db,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "pacemaker potential." Why is the SA node the normal cardiac pacemaker?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **pacemaker potential** is the slow, spontaneous depolarization of the membrane potential during Phase 4 of an autorhythmic cardiac cell (e.g., SA node), occurring in the *absence* of any external stimulus. It is generated primarily by the **"funny current" ($I_f$)** — a slow inward Na⁺ flux through HCN channels — supplemented by T-type Ca²⁺ influx, which gradually raises the membrane potential from about **−60 mV** to the threshold of **−40 mV**. The SA node is the **normal pacemaker** because it possesses the **fastest intrinsic rate of spontaneous depolarization (~100 beats/min)** compared to other latent pacemakers (AV node ~50/min, Purkinje ~30–40/min). Through the principle of **"overdrive suppression,"** the SA node depolarizes surrounding latent cells *before* they can reach threshold on their own, keeping them quiescent. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the significance of the AV nodal delay in cardiac function?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **AV nodal delay (~0.1 s)** is a deliberate slowing of impulse conduction at the atrioventricular junction, caused by the small diameter of AV nodal cells and the sparse distribution of gap junctions. Its physiological significance is threefold: 1. It allows the atria to **completely contract and empty** their contents into the ventricles before ventricular systole begins, optimizing ventricular filling (the "atrial kick," contributing ~30% of ventricular end-diastolic volume). 2. It serves as the **only electrical conduit** between atria and ventricles (the fibrous skeleton insulates the rest), ensuring **one-way** conduction. 3. It acts as a **safety filter**, preventing excessively rapid atrial rates (e.g., during atrial fibrillation) from being conducted 1:1 to the ventricles, which would compromise cardiac output. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Describe the **cardiac conduction system** with a neat diagram. Explain the sequence of impulse propagation from the SA node to the ventricular myocardium. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a graph, explain the **ventricular myocyte action potential**. Discuss its **ionic basis** and explain why cardiac muscle cannot be tetanized. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Cardiac Conduction System (7 marks) The heart contains a specialized **autorhythmic conduction system** comprising the following structures in sequence: 1. **Sinoatrial (SA) Node** — Located in the upper posterolateral wall of the right atrium near the SVC opening. Size ~10 mm × 2 mm. Acts as the **primary pacemaker** with an intrinsic rate of **~100 bpm**. **(1 mark)** 2. **Atrial Internodal Tracts** — Three bundles (Bachmann's, Wenckebach's, Thorel's) conduct the impulse through both atria at ~1 m/s, producing **atrial depolarization (P wave)**. **(1 mark)** 3. **Atrioventricular (AV) Node** — Located in the interatrial septum near the tricuspid valve. Provides the critical **~100 ms delay**. **(1 mark)** 4. **AV Bundle (Bundle of His)** — Penetrates the fibrous skeleton and enters the interventricular septum. **(0.5 marks)** 5. **Right and Left Bundle Branches** — Run along the respective endocardial surfaces of the ventricles. **(0.5 marks)** 6. **Purkinje Fibers** — Large-diameter cells with abundant gap junctions; conduct at **2–4 m/s** (fastest in the heart), spreading activation throughout the ventricular myocardium within ~0.06 s. **(1 mark)** **Sequence Summary:** $$\text{SA Node} \rightarrow \text{Atria} \rightarrow \text{AV Node} \rightarrow \text{Bundle of His} \rightarrow \text{Branches} \rightarrow \text{Purkinje} \rightarrow \text{Ventricles}$$ **Diastasis and Coordination:** The AV delay allows atrial systole to complete before ventricular systole, ensuring optimal cardiac output. The fast Purkinje system ensures the ventricles contract as a **synchronised syncytium** from apex to base for efficient ejection. **(2 marks for diagram + sequencing + functional correlation)** #### Part (b) — Ventricular Action Potential (7 marks) The ventricular myocyte action potential has **5 phases (0–4)**: | Phase | Event | Ionic Basis | Duration | |---|---|---|---| | **0 — Rapid Depolarization** | Sharp upstroke from −90 mV to +30 mV | **Fast voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open | ~1–2 ms | | **1 — Initial Repolarization** | Brief dip | **Transient outward K⁺ current ($I_{to}$)** | ~5–10 ms | | **2 — Plateau** | Sustained depolarization near 0 mV | **L-type Ca²⁺ influx** (slow) balanced by K⁺ efflux | ~200–300 ms | | **3 — Repolarization** | Return to −90 mV | **Delayed rectifier K⁺ currents ($I_K$)** dominate | ~50–100 ms | | **4 — Resting Potential** | Stable at −90 mV | High permeability to K⁺ (inward rectifier $I_{K1}$) | Sustained | **Why Cardiac Muscle Cannot Be Tetanized (2 marks):** The **long absolute refractory period (ARP)** of cardiac muscle (~250 ms) corresponds to the **plateau phase (Phase 2)**, which is sustained by the slow inactivation of L-type Ca²⁺ channels. The ARP **lasts almost as long as the contraction itself**, meaning that: - A second action potential **cannot be elicited** until the cell has nearly finished its mechanical contraction. - The cell must **repolarize and recover excitability** before another action potential is possible. - Therefore, **summation and tetanic contraction are physiologically impossible**, ensuring the heart alternates between **contraction (systole)** and **relaxation (diastole)** — a mechanical necessity for pumping blood. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Question A):** Students frequently: > - Confuse **SA node** (slow-response, Ca²⁺-driven Phase 0) with **ventricular myocytes** (fast-response, Na⁺-driven Phase 0). This costs full marks in part (b). > - Forget to mention **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** when explaining the plateau — a key high-weightage term. > - Omit the **diagram** in part (a) — KTU mandates a labelled diagram for 7-mark questions. Draw even a simple block diagram and label all six components. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Define the **Nernst equation** and calculate the **equilibrium potential for potassium ($E_K$)** at 37 °C given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **ionic basis of the pacemaker potential** in the SA node. How does autonomic nervous system modulation alter heart rate? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Nernst Equation (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential of a single ion species across a semipermeable membrane — i.e., the membrane potential at which there is **no net flux** of that ion, because the electrical driving force exactly balances the chemical concentration gradient. **Mathematical Form (2 marks):** $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$ At body temperature ($T = 310$ K), and converting to log₁₀: $$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}$$ **Calculation for K⁺ (3 marks):** Given: $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM, $z = +1$ $$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$ $$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}(0.02857)$$ $$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.5441)$$ $$\boxed{E_K = -94.96 \text{ mV} \approx -95 \text{ mV}}$$ This value closely matches the resting membrane potential of ventricular myocytes (~−90 mV), confirming that K⁺ permeability is the dominant determinant of the resting potential. #### Part (b) — Pacemaker Potential & Autonomic Modulation (7 marks) **Ionic Basis of Pacemaker Potential (4 marks):** The SA nodal cells **do not have a stable resting potential**. During Phase 4, three key currents produce the spontaneous slow depolarization (the "pacemaker potential"): 1. **"Funny" Current ($I_f$):** Slow inward Na⁺ flux through HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated) channels. Activated on hyperpolarization. **Slope-steepening effect.** 2. **T-type Ca²⁺ Current ($I_{Ca-T}$):** Activated later in Phase 4, contributing the final depolarizing push. 3. **Decay of K⁺ Efflux:** Reduced K⁺ permeability during diastole removes the repolarizing current. Membrane drifts from **−60 mV → −40 mV (threshold)** → **L-type Ca²⁺ channels open → Phase 0 depolarization** of the SA node AP. **Autonomic Modulation (3 marks):** | Division | NT | Receptor | Effect on $I_f$ / Ca²⁺ | Effect on K⁺ | HR Change | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Sympathetic** | Norepinephrine | β₁-adrenergic | ↑↑ (cAMP ↑) | — | **↑ HR** (Tachycardia) | | **Parasympathetic** | Acetylcholine | M₂-muscarinic | ↓ (cAMP ↓) | ↑ K⁺ efflux | **↓ HR** (Bradycardia) | - **Sympathetic stimulation** steepens the slope of Phase 4, **shortening** the time to threshold, hence increasing rate. - **Parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation** flattens the Phase 4 slope, prolonging time to threshold, hence decreasing rate. Vagal tone predominates at rest, reducing the SA rate from intrinsic 100 bpm to a normal resting **60–80 bpm**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Question B):** Students frequently: > - Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) in the *final numerical answer* without converting to mV using $F$. Show the full substitution step-by-step. > - Forget that at body temperature, the constant is **61.5 mV** (not 58 or 59 mV at 20 °C) — KTU specifically tests this value. > - **Omit the logarithm sign** in the final formula or invert the concentration ratio. Carefully re-check: cations = **out/in**, anions = **in/out**. > - Confuse $I_f$ ("funny current," Na⁺) with L-type Ca²⁺ current. $I_f$ operates in mid–late Phase 4; L-type Ca²⁺ drives Phase 0. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize This List** - [x] The heart is **myogenic** — SA node fires automatically; nerves only **modulate**. - [x] Conduction order: **SA → Atria → AV (100 ms delay) → His → Branches → Purkinje (fastest, 2–4 m/s) → Ventricles**. - [x] **SA node** = primary pacemaker; **~100 bpm intrinsic** rate; suppressed by vagal tone to 60–80 bpm at rest. - [x] **SA node AP** is **slow-response** (Ca²⁺-driven Phase 0); no stable Phase 4 — uses **$I_f$ "funny current"** (Na⁺ via HCN channels). - [x] **Ventricular myocyte AP** is **fast-response** (Na⁺-driven Phase 0); has a true **resting potential of −90 mV**; long **plateau (Phase 2)** via L-type Ca²⁺. - [x] **5 phases** of ventricular AP: 0 = Na⁺ in; 1 = K⁺ out (transient); 2 = plateau (Ca²⁺ in ↔ K⁺ out); 3 = K⁺ out (delayed rectifier); 4 = resting (high $I_{K1}$). - [x] **Long absolute refractory period (~250 ms)** prevents **tetanus** — heart must relax to fill. - [x] **Nernst equation at 37 °C:** $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ mV. - [x] **Cations:** ratio is [out]/[in]. **Anions:** flip to [in]/[out]. - [x] **GHK equation** gives the *actual* resting $V_m$ considering multiple ion permeabilities — closer to physiological reality than single-ion Nernst. - [x] **Sympathetic** ↑ HR (β₁, cAMP ↑, steeper Phase 4 slope). - [x] **Parasympathetic** ↓ HR (M₂, ↑ K⁺ efflux, flatter Phase 4). - [x] **ECG correlations:** P wave = atrial depolarization; PR segment = AV nodal delay; QRS = ventricular depolarization; T wave = ventricular repolarization. - [x] **Overdrive suppression** is why the SA node dominates — fastest intrinsic rate suppresses latent pacemakers. - [x] **Heart rate calculation:** HR (bpm) = 60 / RR interval (seconds). - [x] **Clinical relevance:** Pacemakers replace SA node function; defibrillators reset all cells simultaneously; ablation destroys aberrant pathways (WPW, AVNRT, AF triggers). ---

topics.title: Cardiac Action Potential: Resting Membrane Potential and Action Potential of Cardiac Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT415 - QUANTITATIVE PHYSIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cardiac Action Potential: Resting Membrane Potential and Action Potential of Cardiac Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) of Cardiac Cells

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state, transmembrane voltage difference across a cardiac myocyte membrane when the cell is not being stimulated. It arises from the unequal distribution of ions (primarily K⁺, Na⁺, Ca²⁺, and Cl⁻) across the sarcolemma and the differential permeability of the membrane to these ions, maintained actively by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**.

For a typical **ventricular myocyte**, the RMP is approximately **−90 mV** (inside negative with respect to outside), whereas for **sinoatrial (SA) nodal pacemaker cells** it is less negative at approximately **−60 mV**.

### 1.2 Cardiac Action Potential (AP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Cardiac Action Potential** is a rapid, self-propagating, transient change in transmembrane voltage that orchestrates the coordinated sequence of cardiac excitation–contraction coupling. Unlike the brief neuronal action potential (~1 ms), the ventricular cardiac AP exhibits a characteristic **plateau phase (Phase 2)** lasting 200–300 ms, which is the electrical signature that prevents tetanic contraction and ensures proper diastolic filling.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Dam and Reservoir" Analogy:**
> Imagine the cardiac cell membrane as a **hydroelectric dam wall** with selective sluice gates (ion channels) and a pumped-storage turbine (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase).
> - **Resting State:** The reservoir is full of water (high K⁺ inside, ~150 mM). The dam wall holds back the sea (high Na⁺ outside, ~145 mM). The pressure difference is the RMP.
> - **Phase 0 (Depolarization):** Opening the **fast Na⁺ floodgates** lets the sea rush in — voltage shoots up.
> - **Phase 2 (Plateau):** Smaller **Ca²⁺ inflow** is balanced by **K⁺ outflow** — the voltage hovers, like a controlled steady flow.
> - **Phase 3 (Repolarization):** The Ca²⁺ gates close, K⁺ gates stay open, water level falls back.
> - **Phase 4 (Resting):** The turbine (Na⁺/K⁺ pump) restores the original levels using energy (ATP).

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Cardiac Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Body Temperature | T | **310 K (37 °C)** |
| Faraday Constant | F | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| RT/F at 37 °C | — | **26.73 mV** |
| 2.303 RT/F at 37 °C | — | **61.5 mV** |
| Ventricular RMP | Vm | **−90 mV** |
| SA Nodal RMP | Vm | **−60 mV** |
| AP Overshoot (Ventricular) | — | **+30 mV** |
| AP Duration (Ventricular) | APD | **200–300 ms** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nernst Equation — Equilibrium Potential of a Single Ion

The **Nernst Equation** gives the membrane potential at which there is no net electrochemical driving force on a given ion:

$$
E_X \;=\; \frac{RT}{z_X F} \ln\!\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} \;=\; \frac{61.5}{z_X}\,\log_{10}\!\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} \quad \text{(at 37 °C, mV)}
$$

- $E_X$ = equilibrium (Nernst) potential of ion $X$ (mV)
- $z_X$ = valence of ion $X$ (with sign)
- $[X]_o, [X]_i$ = extracellular and intracellular concentrations (mM)

### 2.2 Standard Ionic Equilibrium Potentials in Cardiac Cells

| Ion | $[X]_o$ (mM) | $[X]_i$ (mM) | $z$ | $E_X$ (mV) | Channel Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 4 | 150 | +1 | **−94** | Dominates RMP |
| Na⁺ | 145 | 15 | +1 | **+60** | Phase 0 depolarization |
| Ca²⁺ | 1.2 | 10⁻⁴ | +2 | **+132** | Phase 0/2 plateau |
| Cl⁻ | 120 | 4–30 | −1 | **−70 to −40** | Stabilization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cardiac RMP is close to $E_K$:** At rest, the sarcolemma is far more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺ or Ca²⁺ (the ratio $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} \approx 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ in ventricular myocytes). This is why the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation yields a value near $E_K$.

### 2.3 Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

$$
V_m \;=\; \frac{RT}{F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

where $P_X$ is the relative membrane permeability to ion $X$. This equation is **the** KTU-grade tool for computing the resting potential in cardiac myocytes because it accounts for simultaneous multi-ion permeabilities.

### 2.4 The Five Phases of the Ventricular Action Potential

| Phase | Event | Dominant Current | Ion Movement | Approx. Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Rapid Depolarization | $I_{Na}$ (fast inward Na⁺) | Na⁺ in | 1–2 ms |
| **1** | Initial Repolarization | $I_{to}$ (transient outward K⁺) | K⁺ out | ~10 ms |
| **2** | Plateau | $I_{Ca-L}$ (L-type Ca²⁺) balanced by $I_K$ | Ca²⁺ in / K⁺ out | ~200 ms |
| **3** | Rapid Repolarization | $I_{Kr}, I_{Ks}$ (delayed rectifiers) | K⁺ out | ~50 ms |
| **4** | Resting Potential | $I_{K1}$ (inward rectifier) + Na⁺/K⁺ pump | K⁺ leak | Quiescent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** The **Plateau (Phase 2)** is the *biomedical engineering signature* of the cardiac AP. It results from the **"calcium window current"** where slow inactivation of L-type Ca²⁺ channels runs in parallel with slow activation of delayed-rectifier K⁺ channels. The plateau lengthens the AP from ~1 ms (neuronal) to ~250 ms, ensuring a long refractory period — this is the molecular reason the heart *cannot* be tetanized.

### 2.5 Pacemaker (SA Node) Action Potential — Automaticity

Pacemaker cells **lack a stable Phase 4**. Instead, they exhibit a **slow diastolic depolarization (funny current, $I_f$)** that spontaneously brings the membrane to threshold.

| Phase | Event | Current |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Depolarization | $I_{Ca-L}$ (L-type Ca²⁺ — no fast Na⁺) |
| 3 | Repolarization | $I_K$ (delayed rectifier K⁺) |
| 4 | Spontaneous slow depolarization | $I_f$ (HCN channels, mixed Na⁺/K⁺) + $I_{Ca-T}$ (T-type Ca²⁺) |

### 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **ECG interpretation:** Each phase correlates with a specific ECG wave — Phase 0 = QRS, Phase 2 = ST segment, Phase 3 = T wave.
- **Antiarrhythmic drug design:** Class I (Na⁺ blockers — Phase 0), Class III (K⁺ blockers — Phase 3), Class IV (Ca²⁺ blockers — Phase 2).
- **Pacemaker implants:** Mimic $I_f$ and the SA-node firing rate (~70–80 bpm).
- **Defibrillators:** Reset all cardiac cells to Phase 4 simultaneously.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Nernst | $E_X = \dfrac{61.5}{z_X}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ | Single-ion equilibrium |
| GHK Voltage | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Real resting Vm |
| Nernst–Planck Flux | $J_X = -D_X \dfrac{d[X]}{dx} + \dfrac{z_X F}{RT} D_X [X] \dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | Diffusive + electric flux |
| Conductance Ratio | $g_{Na} : g_K : g_{Ca} : g_{Cl}$ (varies by phase) | Drives phase transitions |
| Resting Conductance | $P_K \gg P_{Na} \Rightarrow V_m \to E_K$ | Justifies RMP ≈ $E_K$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

Starting from electrochemical equilibrium — at equilibrium, the **chemical potential** of ion $X$ on side $i$ equals that on side $o$ (no net flux).

**Step 1.** Chemical potential of ion $X$ on the inside:

$$
\mu_i = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_i + z_X F V_i
$$

**Step 2.** Chemical potential of ion $X$ on the outside:

$$
\mu_o = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]_o + z_X F V_o
$$

**Step 3.** At equilibrium, $\mu_i = \mu_o$. Cancel $\mu^0$:

$$
RT \ln[X]_i + z_X F V_i = RT \ln[X]_o + z_X F V_o
$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange — bring $V$ terms to one side, log terms to the other:

$$
z_X F (V_i - V_o) = RT \ln[X]_o - RT \ln[X]_i
$$

**Step 5.** Factor:

$$
z_X F (V_i - V_o) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for the membrane potential $E_X = V_i - V_o$:

$$
E_X = \frac{RT}{z_X F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 7.** Convert natural log to log base 10 using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$ and substitute $T = 310$ K, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$
E_X = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{z_X \times 96{,}485}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 8.** Numerical evaluation of the prefactor:

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96{,}485} = \frac{5934.2}{96{,}485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 9.** Final working form at 37 °C:

$$
\boxed{\,E_X \;=\; \frac{61.5}{z_X}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right) \;\; \text{(mV)}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺ in a Ventricular Myocyte

**Given:** $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM, $z = +1$, $T = 37$ °C.

**Step 1.** Substitute into the Nernst equation:

$$
E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{150}\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate the log:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{150}\right) = \log_{10}(0.02667) = -1.574
$$

**Step 3.** Multiply:

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.574) = -96.8 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4.** The measured RMP (−90 mV) is slightly *less negative* than $E_K$ because a small Na⁺ and Cl⁻ leak depolarizes the cell by a few mV. The GHK equation captures this.

### 3.3 Worked Example — GHK Calculation of Ventricular RMP

**Given permeabilities and concentrations** (textbook cardiac myocyte values):

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $[K^+]_o$ | 4 mM |
| $[K^+]_i$ | 150 mM |
| $[Na^+]_o$ | 145 mM |
| $[Na^+]_i$ | 15 mM |
| $[Cl^-]_o$ | 120 mM |
| $[Cl^-]_i$ | 4 mM |
| $P_K$ | 1.00 (reference) |
| $P_{Na}$ | 0.04 |
| $P_{Cl}$ | 0.45 |

**Step 1.** Compute numerator (extracellular permeant pool):

$$
N = P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i
$$
$$
N = (1.00)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6
$$

**Step 2.** Compute denominator (intracellular permeant pool):

$$
D = P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o
$$
$$
D = (1.00)(150) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(120) = 150 + 0.6 + 54 = 204.6
$$

**Step 3.** Plug into GHK:

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11.6}{204.6}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the log:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11.6}{204.6}\right) = \log_{10}(0.0567) = -1.246
$$

**Step 5.** Multiply:

$$
V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.246) = -76.6 \text{ mV}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The computed GHK value (−76.6 mV) is more positive than $E_K$ (−96.8 mV) but less negative than the experimentally observed −90 mV. Discrepancy arises because: (a) the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase actively extrudes 3 Na⁺ for every 2 K⁺ it imports, generating an additional electrogenic hyperpolarization of ~10 mV; (b) experimental $P_{Na}$ in true resting conditions is even lower than 0.04. **Adding the ~10 mV electrogenic pump contribution yields −86 to −90 mV**, matching experiment. **This is a KTU-favourite trick question!**

### 3.4 Na⁺ Equilibrium Potential Calculation

**Given:** $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 15$ mM, $z = +1$.

**Step 1.** Substitute:

$$
E_{Na} = \frac{61.5}{+1}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{145}{15}\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate log:

$$
\log_{10}(9.667) = 0.985
$$

**Step 3.** Multiply:

$$
E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 0.985 = +60.6 \text{ mV}
$$

This is why Phase 0 overshoots toward **+30 mV** (it is pulled toward $E_{Na}$ but does not reach it because Na⁺ channels inactivate before equilibrium).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Phase Transitions as Permeability Ratios Change

| Phase | $g_{Na}$ | $g_{Ca}$ | $g_K$ | $V_m$ Trend | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rest (Phase 4) | Very low | Very low | High (via $I_{K1}$) | Constant at $E_K$ | $I_{K1}$ dominates |
| Phase 0 | Sudden huge rise | Low | Low | Rapid rise to +30 mV | $E_{Na}$ = +60 mV pulls Vm up |
| Phase 1 | Inactivates | Low | $I_{to}$ rises briefly | Brief dip | K⁺ efflux |
| Phase 2 | None | Sustained rise | Moderate delayed rise | Plateau near 0 mV | $E_{Ca}$ vs $E_K$ balance |
| Phase 3 | None | Inactivates | $I_{Kr}, I_{Ks}$ peak | Falls to −90 mV | $E_K$ = −94 mV pulls Vm down |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Five Phases of the Ventricular Action Potential

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) of a cardiac ventricular myocyte. State its typical value and name the single most important ion that determines it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **RMP Definition (1 mark):** The Resting Membrane Potential is the steady-state transmembrane potential difference across the sarcolemma of a cardiac myocyte in the absence of stimulation, generated by unequal ionic distribution and selective permeability.
>
> **Typical Value (1 mark):** Approximately **−90 mV** (inside negative) for a ventricular myocyte.
>
> **Dominant Ion (1 mark):** **Potassium (K⁺)** — because at rest, membrane permeability to K⁺ is far greater than to Na⁺ or Ca²⁺, and the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase maintains a steep inward K⁺ gradient. Hence $V_m \approx E_K \approx -94$ mV.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*Why does the cardiac ventricular action potential have a plateau phase (Phase 2), whereas a neuronal action potential does not? Mention the ionic basis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Cardiac plateau exists because (2 marks):** L-type Ca²⁺ channels open slowly during depolarization and remain open for ~200 ms, providing sustained **inward Ca²⁺ current** that is balanced by the **outward delayed-rectifier K⁺ current**. The membrane potential is "clamped" near 0 mV — this is the plateau.
>
> **Neurons lack this because (1 mark):** Neuronal membranes have **no significant L-type Ca²⁺ current** during the AP; repolarization begins immediately after Na⁺ channel inactivation because K⁺ channels activate within 1 ms. Hence the neuronal AP lasts only ~1 ms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each long answer carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation starting from electrochemical equilibrium. Use it to calculate the equilibrium potential of K⁺ in a ventricular myocyte given $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM at 37 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup (2 marks):** Write chemical potentials on either side of the membrane, including the electrical work term $z_X F V$.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition (2 marks):** Set $\mu_i = \mu_o$, cancel $\mu^0$, isolate $V_i - V_o$, and solve:

$$
E_X = \frac{RT}{z_X F}\ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to base-10 (1 mark):**

$$
E_X = \frac{61.5}{z_X}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution for K⁺ (1 mark):**

$$
E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{150}\right) = 61.5 \times (-1.574) \approx -96.8 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The K⁺ equilibrium potential is approximately **−96.8 mV**. The measured RMP of −90 mV is slightly less negative because of small Na⁺ and Cl⁻ leak conductances and the electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺ pump.

---

**(b)** Explain the **ionic basis of the five phases of the ventricular action potential** with the help of a labeled sketch. Why is the long plateau phase critical for cardiac function? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 0 – Rapid Depolarization (1.5 marks):** Voltage-gated fast Na⁺ channels open, Na⁺ rushes in down its electrochemical gradient ($E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV), Vm shoots from −90 mV to +30 mV within 1–2 ms.

**Phase 1 – Initial Repolarization (1 mark):** Na⁺ channels inactivate; transient outward K⁺ current $I_{to}$ opens briefly, causing a small notch in Vm.

**Phase 2 – Plateau (1.5 marks):** L-type Ca²⁺ channels (slow kinetics) open. Sustained Ca²⁺ influx is balanced by K⁺ efflux through delayed rectifiers. Vm hovers near 0 mV for ~200 ms.

**Phase 3 – Rapid Repolarization (1 mark):** Ca²⁺ channels inactivate; $I_{Kr}$ and $I_{Ks}$ (delayed-rectifier K⁺ currents) dominate, driving Vm back toward $E_K$.

**Phase 4 – Resting Potential (1 mark):** Inward rectifier $I_{K1}$ restores and maintains Vm at −90 mV; Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase sustains ionic gradients.

**Physiological Importance of Plateau (1 mark):** The prolonged refractory period (due to long AP) **prevents tetanic contraction** of the heart, ensuring proper diastolic filling between beats. Without the plateau, the heart would behave like a skeletal muscle under rapid stimulation — a fatal outcome.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Using the **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) voltage equation**, calculate the resting membrane potential of a ventricular myocyte. Use the following data: $[K^+]_o = 4$, $[K^+]_i = 150$, $[Na^+]_o = 145$, $[Na^+]_i = 15$, $[Cl^-]_o = 120$, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM; $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1.00 : 0.04 : 0.45$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the GHK equation (1 mark):**

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (2 marks):**

$$
(1.00)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator (2 marks):**

$$
(1.00)(150) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(120) = 150 + 0.6 + 54 = 204.6
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the log and multiply (1 mark):**

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11.6}{204.6}\right) = 61.5 \times (-1.246) = -76.6 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Discuss discrepancy with experiment (1 mark):** The measured RMP is −90 mV. The ~13 mV discrepancy is reconciled by the **electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**, which exports 3 Na⁺ for every 2 K⁺ imported, contributing an additional ~−10 mV hyperpolarization beyond the passive GHK prediction. Together: **−76.6 mV (passive) + (−10 mV pump) ≈ −86 to −90 mV (observed)**.

---

**(b)** Compare the **action potential of a SA-nodal pacemaker cell** with that of a **ventricular myocyte**. Highlight the ionic basis of autorhythmicity in the SA node. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Structural Differences (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ventricular Myocyte | SA-Nodal Pacemaker |
|---|---|---|
| Stable Phase 4 RMP | Yes, −90 mV (held by $I_{K1}$) | No, slowly drifts upward |
| Phase 0 current | Fast Na⁺ ($I_{Na}$) | L-type Ca²⁺ ($I_{Ca-L}$) |
| Overshoot | +30 mV | +10 mV (smaller) |
| Upstroke velocity | Very fast | Slow |
| Plateau | Yes, ~200 ms | No |
| Phase 4 drift | None | Continuous slow depolarization |

**Ionic Basis of Autorhythmicity (3 marks):**

1. **Funny current ($I_f$):** Carried by **HCN channels** (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated). At hyperpolarized potentials, $I_f$ conducts mixed Na⁺/K⁺ influx, slowly depolarizing the cell.
2. **T-type Ca²⁺ current ($I_{Ca-T}$):** Activates near −50 mV and adds inward Ca²⁺ current, further depolarizing the cell.
3. **L-type Ca²⁺ current ($I_{Ca-L}$):** At threshold (~−40 mV), $I_{Ca-L}$ opens decisively, providing Phase 0 upstroke.

**Functional Consequence (1 mark):** The SA node fires **spontaneously at ~70–80 beats per minute** without any external neural input — this is the basis of cardiac **automaticity**. The AV node and Purkinje fibers are latent pacemakers with slower intrinsic rates (40–60 and 20–40 bpm respectively), but are normally suppressed by overdrive pacing from the SA node.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cardiac AP Questions:**
> 1. **Sign errors in the Nernst equation** — Students often forget to put $[X]_o$ in the numerator (not $[X]_i$). One sign slip = wrong equilibrium potential = 3 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/z_X$ factor** — Always divide the 61.5 mV by the valence. For Ca²⁺, this halves the voltage, but Ca²⁺'s huge concentration gradient compensates.
> 3. **Confusing cardiac and neuronal APs** — Writing "Phase 0 is due to Ca²⁺" for a ventricular myocyte is a **factual error** costing full marks. Reserve Ca²⁺-mediated Phase 0 for **pacemaker cells only**.
> 4. **Ignoring the Na⁺/K⁺ pump contribution** — Pure GHK gives only ~−77 mV; the pump's ~−10 mV electrogenic contribution is required to reach −90 mV. Students who skip this lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the labeled diagram** — Even for short derivations, KTU examiners expect a sketch of the AP waveform with phases labeled. A clear diagram often earns 1–2 easy marks.
> 6. **Units!** — mV must accompany every numerical answer. $E_K = -96.8$ is incomplete; write $E_K \approx -96.8$ mV.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cardiac AP & RMP**

- **RMP of ventricular myocyte = −90 mV**, dominated by **K⁺** (Na⁺/K⁺ pump + $I_{K1}$ leak).
- **RMP of SA node = −60 mV**; less negative because of smaller $I_{K1}$ and continuous $I_f$.
- **Nernst equation** at 37 °C: $E_X = (61.5/z_X)\log_{10}([X]_o/[X]_i)$ mV.
- **GHK equation** gives the multi-ion passive RMP; the **Na⁺/K⁺ pump** adds ~−10 mV electrogenic hyperpolarization.
- **Standard $E$ values:** $E_K \approx -94$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV, $E_{Ca} \approx +132$ mV.
- **5 Phases of Ventricular AP:**
  - Phase 0: Na⁺ in (fast) — fast upstroke
  - Phase 1: K⁺ out (transient) — early notch
  - Phase 2: Ca²⁺ in balanced by K⁺ out — **plateau (signature!)**
  - Phase 3: K⁺ out (delayed rectifiers) — repolarization
  - Phase 4: K⁺ leak — resting
- **Plateau is critical** — prevents tetany, ensures diastolic filling; ECG ST segment corresponds to it.
- **Pacemaker AP has no Phase 1 or stable Phase 4.** Phase 0 uses **Ca²⁺ (L-type)**, Phase 4 drift driven by **$I_f$ (HCN)** and **$I_{Ca-T}$**.
- **SA node** sets heart rate at **70–80 bpm** — overdrive suppression keeps subordinate pacemakers quiescent.
- **Antiarrhythmic drug classes** map directly onto AP phases (Class I → Na⁺, Class III → K⁺, Class IV → Ca²⁺).
- **ECG correlation:** QRS ↔ Phase 0; ST ↔ Phase 2; T-wave ↔ Phase 3.
- **Clinical relevance:** Long-QT syndrome = defective $I_{Kr}$ Phase 3 → fatal arrhythmias. Brugada syndrome = defective Na⁺ Phase 0.

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Autonomic Nervous System (ANS) — Formal Definition

The **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)** is the division of the Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) that innervates **smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands**, regulating involuntary bodily functions such as heart rate, digestion, respiration, pupillary response, urination, and sexual arousal. Unlike the Somatic Nervous System (SNS-motor), the ANS uses a **two-neuron efferent chain** — a *preganglionic* neuron (originating in the CNS) synapsing onto a *postganglionic* neuron (in an autonomic ganglion) that finally innervates the target effector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> The ANS is the visceral efferent (motor) nervous system that subconsciously regulates visceral functions. It is functionally partitioned into the **Sympathetic** (Thoracolumbar, T1–L2), **Parasympathetic** (Craniosacral, CN III, VII, IX, X + S2–S4), and **Enteric** (gut-intrinsic) subdivisions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Body's Automatic Dual-Clutch System"

Imagine your body as a **modern hybrid car** driving through traffic:

- The **Somatic Nervous System** = the *steering wheel and gear stick* — direct, conscious control.
- The **Sympathetic Nervous System (SNS)** = the **accelerator / "fight-or-flight" mode** — revs the engine, increases fuel injection, dilates exhaust (pupils dilate), suppresses non-essential systems (digestion slows).
- The **Parasympathetic Nervous System (PNS)** = the **brake / "rest-and-digest" mode** — idles the engine at the signal, conserves fuel, runs cleaning cycles (digestion active, heart rate low).
- The **Enteric Nervous System** = the **onboard fuel-injection ECU** — a small, semi-autonomous computer controlling local gut traffic independently of the driver.

Both accelerator (SNS) and brake (PNS) cables pull on the *same* engine (heart, glands, smooth muscle) in **reciprocal or complementary fashion**. The body's automatic "tuning" depends on which pedal is pressed harder.

## 1.3 Key Terminology — KTU Exam Vocabulary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Preganglionic neuron | 1st efferent neuron; cell body in CNS, axon to autonomic ganglion |
| Postganglionic neuron | 2nd efferent neuron; cell body in ganglion, axon to effector |
| Autonomic ganglion | Cluster of postganglionic neuron cell bodies |
| Cholinergic | Releases **Acetylcholine (ACh)** as neurotransmitter |
| Adrenergic | Releases **Norepinephrine (NE / Noradrenaline)** as neurotransmitter |
| Agonist | Drug that mimics the natural neurotransmitter |
| Antagonist | Drug that blocks the receptor |
| Visceroceptor | Sensory receptor monitoring visceral state (stretch, chemoreception) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Anchor for Memory:** The ANS transmits signals at **1–3 m/s** (preganglionic B-fibers) and **0.5–2 m/s** (postganglionic C-fibers) — orders of magnitude slower than somatic A-α fibers (**80–120 m/s**), reflecting the *autonomic*, non-emergency nature of most visceral traffic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of the ANS

The efferent pathway follows a **rigorous two-step chain** for every command:

1. **Preganglionic neuron** — cell body in the CNS (lateral horn of spinal cord or brainstem nucleus).
2. **Synapse in autonomic ganglion** — synapse point outside the CNS.
3. **Postganglionic neuron** — projects to the target organ (effector).
4. **Neuro-effector junction** — releases neurotransmitter onto receptor of effector cell.

**The "Why" of two neurons:** Allows **divergence** (one preganglionic fiber synapses onto many postganglionic fibers, amplifying the signal) and **integration** at the ganglion (modulation before reaching the organ).

## 2.2 Sympathetic vs Parasympathetic — KTU Comparative Logic

| Feature | Sympathetic (SNS) | Parasympathetic (PNS) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Thoracolumbar (T1–L2) | Craniosacral (CN III, VII, IX, X + S2–S4) |
| Ganglia location | Paravertebral chain (close to spinal cord) | Terminal (in/near effector organ) |
| Preganglionic fiber | Short | Long |
| Postganglionic fiber | Long | Short |
| Neurotransmitter (ganglion) | Acetylcholine | Acetylcholine |
| Neurotransmitter (effector) | **Norepinephrine** (sweat glands → ACh) | **Acetylcholine** |
| Receptor on effector | α1, α2, β1, β2, β3 | Muscarinic (M1–M5) |
| Dominant receptor at ganglion | **Nicotinic (Nn)** | **Nicotinic (Nn)** |
| Mass discharge | Yes (mass sympathetic activation) | No (discrete, organ-specific) |
| Function | Catabolic (fight-or-flight) | Anabolic (rest-and-digest) |

## 2.3 Receptor Pharmacology — The KTU Master Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mnemonic for adrenergic receptors: **"**A**BC of Adrenergic"** — **α** (vasoconstriction), **β1** (**B**eat — heart), **β2** (**B**ronchi — relax/dilate), **β3** (**B**ladders of fat — lipolysis).

| Receptor | Location | Effect of Stimulation |
|---|---|---|
| α1 | Vascular smooth muscle, radial iris muscle | Vasoconstriction, mydriasis |
| α2 | Presynaptic terminals, pancreatic β-cells | ↓ NE release, ↓ insulin |
| β1 | Heart (SA node, AV node, myocardium) | ↑ HR, ↑ contractility, ↑ conduction |
| β2 | Bronchial smooth muscle, vascular SM (skeletal muscle), uterus | Bronchodilation, vasodilation, uterine relaxation |
| β3 | Adipose tissue | Lipolysis |
| M1 | Gastric parietal cells, CNS | ↑ Gastric acid secretion |
| M2 | Heart (SA & AV node) | ↓ HR, ↓ conduction |
| M3 | Smooth muscle, glands, eye | Bronchoconstriction, gland secretion, miosis, GI motility |
| Nn (Nicotinic neuronal) | All autonomic ganglia, adrenal medulla | Ganglionic transmission |
| Nm (Nicotinic muscle) | Skeletal muscle NMJ | Muscle contraction (NOT autonomic) |

## 2.4 Organ-Level Effects Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Organ | Sympathetic Effect | Parasympathetic Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Heart (rate) | ↑ (β1) | ↓ (M2) |
| Heart (contractility) | ↑ (β1) | ↓ (slight) |
| Bronchi | Bronchodilation (β2) | Bronchoconstriction (M3) |
| Pupil | Mydriasis (α1) | Miosis (M3) |
| GI motility | ↓ (α2, β2) | ↑ (M3) |
| GI secretion | ↓ | ↑ (M3) |
| Bladder (detrusor) | Relaxation (β3) | Contraction (M3) |
| Bladder (sphincter) | Contraction (α1) | Relaxation |
| Blood vessels (skin/splanchnic) | Constriction (α1) | No innervation |
| Sweat glands | Secretion (ACh via sympathetic) | — |
| Adrenal medulla | Direct secretion of E + NE | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG, HRV (Heart Rate Variability) analysis uses **sympathovagal balance** — LF/HF ratio (Low Frequency / High Frequency power in frequency-domain HRV).
- **Pharmacology:** Beta-blockers (β1 antagonists), anticholinergics, alpha-blockers — directly modulate ANS.
- **Neural Engineering:** Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) implants exploit parasympathetic pathways to treat epilepsy and depression.
- **Anesthesiology:** Quantitative understanding of ANS drives choice of pre-medication (e.g., atropine blocks M2 → tachycardia).

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary / Threshold Note:** Sympathetic firing frequency modulates effector output logarithmically over a physiological range of **0.5–20 Hz**; below ~0.5 Hz, basal tone only is maintained.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Biosynthesis of Norepinephrine (Step-by-Step)

Norepinephrine (NE) synthesis from dietary amino acid **tyrosine** involves **four enzymatic conversions**. Each step is shown explicitly with chemistry.

**Step 1 — Hydroxylation (Tyrosine → L-DOPA)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tyrosine} + \text{O}_2 + \text{Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4)} &\xrightarrow{\text{Tyrosine Hydroxylase (TH, rate-limiting)}} \text{L-DOPA} \\
&\quad + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{BH2}
\end{aligned}
$$
*(Conversion logic: TH introduces an –OH group at the 3-position of the phenyl ring, consuming one oxygen atom and cofactor BH4.)*

**Step 2 — Decarboxylation (L-DOPA → Dopamine)**

$$
\text{L-DOPA} \xrightarrow{\text{DOPA Decarboxylase (AADC)}} \text{Dopamine} + \text{CO}_2
$$
*(Conversion logic: AADC removes the carboxyl group as CO2, leaving the primary amine.)*

**Step 3 — β-Hydroxylation (Dopamine → Norepinephrine)**

$$
\text{Dopamine} + \text{Ascorbate} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Dopamine }\beta\text{-Hydroxylase (DBH)}} \text{Norepinephrine} + \text{Dehydroascorbate} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
*(Conversion logic: DBH adds an –OH group to the β-carbon of the ethylamine side chain. This enzyme resides in **vesicular storage granules** of postganglionic sympathetic neurons and adrenal medulla chromaffin cells.)*

**Step 4 — N-methylation (in adrenal medulla only)**

$$
\text{Norepinephrine} + \text{SAM} \xrightarrow{\text{Phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase (PNMT)}} \text{Epinephrine} + \text{SAH}
$$
*(Conversion logic: SAM = S-adenosyl-methionine donates a methyl group; SAH = S-adenosyl-homocysteine is the byproduct. PNMT is induced by **cortisol** in adrenal medulla, so **epinephrine synthesis is adrenal-exclusive**.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rate-limiting enzyme is Tyrosine Hydroxylase (TH)** — clinically, the drug **Metyrosine (α-methyl-p-tyrosine)** inhibits TH and is used in pheochromocytoma management.

## 3.2 Biosynthesis of Acetylcholine (Step-by-Step)

$$
\text{Choline} + \text{Acetyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{Choline Acetyltransferase (ChAT)}} \text{Acetylcholine (ACh)} + \text{CoA}
$$
*(Conversion logic: ChAT transfers the acetyl group from acetyl-CoA onto the hydroxyl of choline in the neuronal cytoplasm; ACh is then packaged into synaptic vesicles via the **VAChT (Vesicular ACh Transporter)**.)*

## 3.3 Termination of Action — Quantitative Matrix

| Neurotransmitter | Termination Mechanism | Quantitative Detail |
|---|---|---|
| Norepinephrine | Reuptake (NET) ≈ **80–90%**, then **MAO** (mitochondria) + **COMT** (cytoplasm) | Plasma half-life ≈ **1–3 min** |
| Acetylcholine | Hydrolysis by **Acetylcholinesterase (AChE)** at synaptic cleft | AChE catalytic rate ≈ **10,000 ACh/s per active site** (one of fastest enzymes known) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Signal Transduction of Key Receptors

| Receptor | G-protein | 2nd Messenger | Final Cellular Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| α1 | Gq | ↑ IP3 / DAG / Ca2+ | Smooth muscle contraction |
| α2 | Gi | ↓ cAMP | ↓ NE release, ↓ insulin |
| β1, β2, β3 | Gs | ↑ cAMP | ↑ PKA activity → target phosphorylation |
| M1, M3 | Gq | ↑ IP3 / DAG / Ca2+ | Smooth muscle contraction, gland secretion |
| M2 | Gi | ↓ cAMP | ↓ HR, ↓ AV conduction |

## 3.5 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) Quantitative Relationship

This is a direct quantitative-physiology derivation relevant to PEBMT415:

$$
\text{LF/HF Ratio} = \frac{\int_{0.04\,\text{Hz}}^{0.15\,\text{Hz}} P(f)\,df}{\int_{0.15\,\text{Hz}}^{0.40\,\text{Hz}} P(f)\,df}
$$
*(Conversion logic: LF (Low Frequency) band reflects mixed sympathetic + parasympathetic activity; HF (High Frequency) band reflects pure **parasympathetic (vagal) tone**. A high LF/HF ratio indicates sympathetic dominance; a low ratio indicates parasympathetic dominance — used clinically to assess autonomic balance in diabetic autonomic neuropathy and post-MI risk stratification.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Sympathovagal Balance Index (SVI):**
$$
\text{SVI} = \frac{\text{LF Power (ms}^2\text{)}}{\text{HF Power (ms}^2\text{)}}
$$
> Normal resting SVI ≈ **1.0–2.0** in healthy adults; values >4 indicate pathological sympathetic overactivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ANS Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CNS1["Central Nervous System Brainstem and Spinal Cord"] --> PRE1["Preganglionic Neuron Cell body in CNS"]
    PRE1 -->|Acetylcholine via Nn receptor| GANGLIA["Autonomic Ganglion Outside CNS"]
    GANGLIA --> POST1["Postganglionic Neuron Cell body in ganglion"]
    POST1 -->|Sympathetic NE via adrenergic receptor| EFFECTOR1["Effector Smooth Muscle Cardiac Muscle Glands"]
    POST1 -->|Parasympathetic ACh via muscarinic receptor| EFFECTOR1
    EFFECTOR1 --> FEEDBACK["Afferent Visceroceptor Signal to CNS"]
    FEEDBACK --> CNS1
    CNS1 --> CORTEX["Higher Centers Hypothalamus Limbic Cortex"]
    CORTEX --> CNS1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sympathetic vs Parasympathetic Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYMPATHETIC
        THOR["Thoracolumbar T1 to L2"] --> PARA["Paravertebral Chain Ganglion Short preganglionic"]
        PARA --> POST_S["Long postganglionic fiber"]
        POST_S -->|NE| ORG_S["Target Organ alpha and beta receptors"]
    end
    subgraph PARASYMPATHETIC
        CRAN["Craniosacral CN III VII IX X S2 to S4"] --> TERM["Terminal Ganglion Near or in organ"]
        TERM --> POST_P["Short postganglionic fiber"]
        POST_P -->|ACh| ORG_P["Target Organ muscarinic receptors M1 to M5"]
    end
    THOR -->|Antagonistic balance| ORG_S
    CRAN -->|Antagonistic balance| ORG_P
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Neurotransmitter Synthesis Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TYR["Tyrosine From diet and liver synthesis"] -->|Tyrosine Hydroxylase RATE LIMITING| LDOPA["L DOPA"]
    LDOPA -->|DOPA Decarboxylase| DA["Dopamine"]
    DA -->|Dopamine Beta Hydroxylase| NE["Norepinephrine Vesicle storage"]
    NE -->|PNMT Adrenal medulla only| EPI["Epinephrine Adrenal medulla only"]
    CHO["Choline From diet and reuptake"] -->|Choline Acetyltransferase ChAT| ACH["Acetylcholine"]
    ACH -->|Acetylcholinesterase AChE rapid hydrolysis| CHO
    ACH -->|Rapid hydrolysis| CHOLINE_BACK["Choline and Acetate Recycled"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Autonomic Reflex Arc (Baroreceptor Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STIM["BP Rise Detected by Carotid Sinus Baroreceptor"] --> AFF["Afferent Vagal Fiber CN IX CN X"]
    AFF --> NTS["Nucleus Tractus Solitarius Medulla"]
    NTS --> INTEGRATE["Cardiovascular Center Integration"]
    INTEGRATE -->|Increase| PNS_OUT["Parasympathetic Output CN X Vagus"]
    INTEGRATE -->|Decrease| SNS_OUT["Sympathetic Output Decrease"]
    PNS_OUT --> SA["SA Node Heart"]
    SNS_OUT --> SA
    SA --> RESPONSE["Heart Rate Decreases BP Normalizes"]
    RESPONSE -.Negative feedback.-> STIM
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the autonomic nervous system. List its three divisions with their anatomical origins.**

**Model Answer:**
The autonomic nervous system (ANS) is the division of the peripheral nervous system that regulates involuntary visceral functions through innervation of smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.

- **Sympathetic** – Thoracolumbar outflow (T1–L2)
- **Parasympathetic** – Craniosacral outflow (CN III, VII, IX, X + S2–S4)
- **Enteric** – Intrinsic ganglia of the gut wall (myenteric + submucosal plexuses)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between adrenergic and cholinergic neurotransmission with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Adrenergic** – Releases **norepinephrine** (or epinephrine) onto α/β receptors; e.g., sympathetic postganglionic fibers to the heart (β1 → ↑ HR).
- **Cholinergic** – Releases **acetylcholine** onto muscarinic or nicotinic receptors; e.g., parasympathetic postganglionic fibers to the heart (M2 → ↓ HR).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the organization of the sympathetic nervous system with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Origin:** Lateral horn of spinal cord segments T1–L2 (intermediolateral cell column).
2. **Preganglionic fibers:** Myelinated B-fibers exit via ventral root → white ramus communicans → synapse in sympathetic chain (paravertebral ganglion).
3. **Synapse options:**
   - **Same-level** chain ganglion
   - **Ascending/descending** chain ganglion
   - **Prevertebral ganglia** (celiac, superior mesenteric, inferior mesenteric)
   - **Adrenal medulla** (direct innervation — chromaffin cells are modified postganglionic neurons)
4. **Postganglionic fibers:** Unmyelinated C-fibers travel via **gray ramus communicans** to spinal nerves or directly to viscera.
5. **Neurotransmitters:** ACh at ganglion (Nn); **NE** at effector (α, β); ACh at sweat glands (sympathetic but cholinergic exception).

---

**(b)** Explain the biosynthesis, storage, release, and termination of norepinephrine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Biosynthesis:** Tyrosine → L-DOPA (Tyrosine Hydroxylase, rate-limiting) → Dopamine (DOPA decarboxylase) → NE (Dopamine β-hydroxylase in vesicles).
2. **Storage:** NE packaged into **chromaffin granules / synaptic vesicles** via VMAT transporter; protected from MAO.
3. **Release:** Action potential → Ca²⁺ influx → vesicle fusion → exocytosis of NE into synaptic cleft.
4. **Termination (three routes):**
   - **Reuptake (80–90%)** into presynaptic terminal via **NET (Norepinephrine Transporter)**.
   - **Enzymatic degradation:** MAO (mitochondria) → DOMA; COMT (cytoplasm) → VMA; final metabolite excreted in urine.
   - **Diffusion** away from cleft (minor).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative for Internal Choice**

**(a)** Compare the effects of sympathetic and parasympathetic stimulation on the heart, eye, GI tract, and urinary bladder. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Organ | Sympathetic | Parasympathetic |
|---|---|---|
| **Heart** | ↑ HR (β1), ↑ contractility, ↑ AV conduction (β1) | ↓ HR (M2), ↓ AV conduction (M2) |
| **Eye** | Mydriasis — radial muscle contraction (α1); far vision accommodation (β) | Miosis — sphincter pupillae (M3); near vision via ciliary muscle contraction (M3) |
| **GI tract** | ↓ Motility (α2, β2); ↓ secretion (α2); sphincter contraction (α1) | ↑ Motility (M3); ↑ secretion (M3); sphincter relaxation |
| **Urinary bladder** | Detrusor **relaxation** (β3); internal sphincter **contraction** (α1) → urine retention | Detrusor **contraction** (M3); internal sphincter **relaxation** → micturition |

---

**(b)** Describe the cholinergic receptors — their subtypes, signaling mechanisms, and clinical significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Muscarinic (M1–M5)** — GPCR family:
   - **M1, M3, M5** → Gq → ↑ IP3 / DAG / Ca²⁺ → smooth muscle contraction, gland secretion.
   - **M2, M4** → Gi → ↓ cAMP → ↓ HR (cardiac M2).
2. **Nicotinic** — Ligand-gated ion channels (pentameric):
   - **Nn (Neuronal)** – at autonomic ganglia and adrenal medulla.
   - **Nm (Muscle)** – at skeletal NMJ.
3. **Clinical significance:**
   - **Atropine** (M-antagonist) — pre-op to dry secretions, treat bradycardia.
   - **Bethanechol** (M-agonist) — post-op urinary retention.
   - **Nicotine** — activates Nn, then desensitizes ganglia.
   - **Myasthenia gravis** — anti-Nm antibodies treated with AChE inhibitors (neostigmine).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cholinergic exception:** Sympathetic fibers to **sweat glands and some skeletal muscle vasculature** release ACh, not NE. Students who blindly write "all sympathetic = NE" lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Misidentifying ganglia:** Confusing prevertebral (celiac, etc.) with paravertebral chain ganglia — examiner specifically tests *location-based* identification.
> 3. **Skipping Tyrosine Hydroxylase** as the rate-limiting enzyme in NE synthesis — a **3-mark loss** if omitted in a 14-marker.
> 4. **Confusing α and β effects:** Writing "β1 causes bronchodilation" instead of **β2** — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Failing to draw/label a pathway diagram** in part-(a) of Part B — KTU mandates a diagram for full marks on anatomy questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ANS = visceral efferent system** controlling smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, glands.
- **Two-neuron chain:** Preganglionic (in CNS) → Postganglionic (in ganglion).
- **Sympathetic origin:** Thoracolumbar (T1–L2); long postganglionic; releases **NE**; receptors **α1, α2, β1, β2, β3**.
- **Parasympathetic origin:** Craniosacral (CN III, VII, IX, X + S2–S4); short postganglionic; releases **ACh**; receptors **M1–M5**.
- **All autonomic ganglia use ACh** on **Nn (nicotinic)** receptors.
- **Adrenal medulla = modified sympathetic ganglion**; releases **E (80%) + NE (20%)** directly into blood.
- **Sweat glands = sympathetic but cholinergic** (muscarinic) — unique exception.
- **NE biosynthesis:** Tyrosine → L-DOPA → Dopamine → NE → (E in adrenal); **TH is rate-limiting**.
- **ACh hydrolysis:** AChE is among the fastest enzymes known.
- **Termination of NE:** Reuptake (~85%) → MAO + COMT degradation.
- **Heart:** SNS ↑ HR/contractility (β1); PNS ↓ HR (M2).
- **Eye:** SNS → mydriasis (α1); PNS → miosis + accommodation (M3).
- **Bladder:** SNS → retention; PNS → micturition.
- **HRV / LF-HF ratio:** Quantitative measure of autonomic balance used in clinical diagnostics.
- **Drug examples:** Atropine (M-block), Propranolol (β-block), Phenylephrine (α1-agonist), Bethanechol (M-agonist).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ionic Basis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT415 - QUANTITATIVE PHYSIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ionic Basis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ionic Basis** of cellular excitability refers to the quantitative description of how unequal distributions of ions (primarily **Na⁺**, **K⁺**, **Cl⁻**, and **Ca²⁺**) across a biological membrane, combined with selective ion permeability, generate an **electrochemical gradient** that produces the **resting membrane potential (RMP)** and the **action potential (AP)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the ionic basis is mathematically formalized through the **Nernst Equation**, the **Nernst-Planck Equation**, and the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The ionic basis is the cornerstone of electrophysiology. A student must master (i) Nernst potentials for individual ions, (ii) the GHK equation for combined membrane potential, and (iii) the role of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump in maintaining ionic gradients.

### 1.2 Key Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|:------:|:-----:|:----:|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Body Temperature | $T$ | **310** (≈37°C) | K |
| $\frac{RT}{F}$ at 37°C | — | **0.02671** | V (≈26.71 mV) |
| $\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}$ at 37°C | — | **0.0615** | V (≈61.5 mV) |

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical constant **61.5 mV** is the "magic number" you will see repeatedly. It comes from $2.303 \times \frac{RT}{F}$ at body temperature, and converts natural logarithms to base-10 logarithms in the Nernst equation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Battery" Model

Imagine a **water tank on a hill** (high concentration of ions outside the cell) connected through a **one-way valve** (a selectively permeable ion channel) to a **lower tank** (low concentration inside the cell). Water flows downhill, generating potential energy. If you attach a **turbine** to the flow, you can generate electricity.

Similarly, in a living cell:
- **Ions** (Na⁺, K⁺) are the "water" — stored at different concentrations inside and outside the cell.
- **Ion channels** are the "valves" — they open and close to allow specific ions through.
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ pump** is the "electric pump" on the hill that continuously pumps ions back uphill, maintaining the gradient.
- The **membrane potential** (≈ –70 mV inside) is the "electricity" generated by the ionic flow.

This analogy explains why nerve cells act like tiny biological batteries: **stored chemical energy (ion gradients) → electrical energy (membrane potential) → signals (action potentials).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Ionic Basis

**Pillar 1: Chemical Gradient (Concentration Difference)**
- Ions are not equally distributed across the cell membrane.
- Example: $[K^+]_{in} = 155$ mM vs $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM → K⁺ wants to diffuse **outward**.
- Example: $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$ mM vs $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM → Na⁺ wants to diffuse **inward**.

**Pillar 2: Electrical Gradient (Membrane Potential)**
- The inside of a resting cell is electrically **negative** (≈ –70 mV) compared to the outside.
- This negative interior **repels** anions and **attracts** cations, opposing the chemical gradient for some ions.

**Pillar 3: Electrochemical Equilibrium (Nernst Potential)**
- For each ion, there exists a specific membrane voltage at which the **electrical force** exactly balances the **chemical force**.
- At this voltage, the net flux of that ion is **zero** — this is the **Nernst (Equilibrium) Potential**, denoted $E_{ion}$.

### 2.2 Why is the Resting Potential ≈ –70 mV?

The RMP is **not** a single Nernst potential — it is a **weighted average** of multiple ion equilibria, weighted by their **relative permeabilities**. Since the resting membrane is **~25–100 times more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺**, the RMP lies close to $E_K$ (≈ –97 mV) but is shifted toward $E_{Na}$ (≈ +66 mV) by the small Na⁺ leak. The result: **RMP ≈ –70 mV**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Permeability at Rest:** "**K-leak** keeps the cell quiet" — the resting membrane is dominated by K⁺ permeability.

### 2.3 The Action Potential — A Brief Ionic Story

| Phase | Voltage | Primary Ion Movement | Channel Responsible |
|:-----:|:-------:|:--------------------:|:-------------------:|
| Resting | –70 mV | K⁺ leak outward | Leak K⁺ channels |
| Depolarization (rising) | –70 → +30 mV | Na⁺ rushes **in** | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels |
| Repolarization (falling) | +30 → –70 mV | K⁺ rushes **out** | Voltage-gated K⁺ channels |
| Hyperpolarization (undershoot) | –70 → –80 mV | Continued K⁺ efflux | K⁺ channels (slow to close) |
| Return to Rest | –80 → –70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores gradients | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:------------|:-----:|
| 1 | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst Equation (natural log form) | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst Equation (base-10 form at 37°C) | mV |
| 3 | $E_{ion} = -\frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$ | Alternative form (for anion convention) | mV |
| 4 | $J_{ion} = -D \frac{dC}{dx} - \frac{zF D C}{RT} \frac{dV}{dx}$ | Nernst-Planck Equation (total ion flux) | mol/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation | Volts |
| 6 | $V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | GHK Equation (base-10, 37°C, mV) | mV |
| 7 | $I_{pump} = 3 \text{ Na}^+ \text{ out} / 2 \text{ K}^+ \text{ in per ATP}$ | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase stoichiometry | ions/cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA CONVENTION:** In the Nernst equation, $z$ is the **valence** of the ion (+1 for K⁺, +1 for Na⁺, –1 for Cl⁻, +2 for Ca²⁺). The **sign of $z$** directly affects the sign of $E_{ion}$. For Cl⁻ ($z = -1$), many textbooks use the inverted concentration ratio to keep $E_{Cl}$ negative — always check your textbook's convention!

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Ionic Basis

- **ECG / EEG / EMG Signal Processing:** The ionic basis underpins the biopotential signals measured in clinical diagnostics. The dipole model of the heart is built directly on the Nernst and GHK equations.
- **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Devices like the cochlear implant and deep brain stimulators manipulate ionic currents — design specifications are based on Nernst potentials.
- **Drug Delivery & Ion Channel Therapeutics:** Drugs like lidocaine, amiodarone, and diuretics target specific ion channels. Quantitative physiology provides the framework for drug-receptor dose-response modeling.
- **Biosensor Design:** Ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) used in clinical analyzers (e.g., blood gas analyzers) are designed using the Nernst equation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

**Starting Premise:** When an ion is at electrochemical equilibrium across a membrane, the **net flux** of that ion is zero. This means the **electrochemical potential** ($\mu$) must be the same on both sides of the membrane.

**Step 1: Define the Electrochemical Potential**

The electrochemical potential of an ion on the outside of the membrane is:

$$\mu_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln([ion]_{out}) + zFV_{out}$$

The electrochemical potential on the inside is:

$$\mu_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln([ion]_{in}) + zFV_{in}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition**

At equilibrium, $\mu_{out} = \mu_{in}$ (no net driving force on the ion):

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln([ion]_{out}) + zFV_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln([ion]_{in}) + zFV_{in}$$

**Step 3: Cancel the Standard Chemical Potential $\mu^0$**

Both sides contain the same standard chemical potential term, which cancels:

$$RT \ln([ion]_{out}) + zFV_{out} = RT \ln([ion]_{in}) + zFV_{in}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate the Voltage Difference**

Move all voltage terms to one side and all concentration terms to the other:

$$zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln([ion]_{out}) - RT \ln([ion]_{in})$$

Factor the right-hand side using the logarithm rule $\ln A - \ln B = \ln(A/B)$:

$$zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Membrane Potential $V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$**

Divide both sides by $zF$:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 6: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm (Engineering Form)**

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$V_m = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

At $T = 310$ K (body temperature), $\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615$ V = **61.5 mV**.

$$\boxed{\,E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Nernst-Planck Equation

**Starting Premise:** The total flux $J$ of an ion across a membrane has two contributions: (i) **diffusion** down the concentration gradient, and (ii) **electromigration** down the electrical gradient.

**Step 1: Write the Diffusion Flux (Fick's First Law)**

The diffusive flux is proportional to the negative concentration gradient:

$$J_{diff} = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s) and $C$ is the concentration (mol/m³).

**Step 2: Write the Electromigration Flux**

The force on an ion in an electric field is $F_{elec} = -zF \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}$. The ion's drift velocity is $v = u \cdot F_{elec}$, where $u$ is the **mobility** (m²/(V·s)). The resulting flux is:

$$J_{mig} = -zFC u \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3: Relate Mobility to the Diffusion Coefficient (Einstein Relation)**

The Einstein-Smoluchowski relation states:

$$u = \frac{D}{RT}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Einstein Relation into the Migration Flux**

$$J_{mig} = -zFC \cdot \frac{D}{RT} \cdot \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -\frac{zFDC}{RT} \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5: Sum the Two Fluxes**

The total Nernst-Planck flux is the sum of diffusion and migration:

$$\boxed{\,J_{ion} = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} - \frac{zFDC}{RT} \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\,}$$

This equation is the **constitutive equation** for ion transport in electrophysiology. It forms the basis for deriving the GHK equation by integrating across the membrane thickness.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation

**Starting Premise:** Assume (i) constant electric field across the membrane, (ii) constant ion concentrations at the boundaries, and (iii) three permeant ions: K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻.

**Step 1: Integrate the Nernst-Planck Equation Across the Membrane**

For a single ion with constant field, the flux from outside (at $x = 0$, $C = C_{out}$, $V = 0$) to inside (at $x = \delta$, $C = C_{in}$, $V = V_m$) is:

$$J = P \cdot \frac{zF V_m / RT}{1 - \exp(-zFV_m / RT)} \left( C_{in} - C_{out} \exp(-zFV_m / RT) \right)$$

where $P = D/\delta$ is the permeability coefficient.

**Step 2: Apply the Steady-State Condition**

At steady state, the total membrane current must be zero (no net charge buildup by a single ion). Sum the currents from K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻:

$$I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute and Simplify**

Using $I_{ion} = zFJ_{ion}$ for each species, and setting the sum to zero:

$$P_K[K^+]_{in} - P_K[K^+]_{out} \cdot e^{-FV_m/RT} \text{ (terms) + ...} = 0$$

After algebraic simplification (which is tedious but standard in electrophysiology textbooks), the result is:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 4: Convert to the Engineering (Base-10) Form at 37°C**

$$\boxed{\,V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right) \text{ mV}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Cl⁻ "inverted" in the GHK equation?** Because Cl⁻ is a negative ion ($z = -1$), flipping the concentration ratio in the formula keeps the **sign convention consistent** with the way permeability appears in the numerator and denominator. This is a frequently tested subtlety in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Given Data (Typical Mammalian Neuron):**
- $[K^+]_{in} = 155$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$ mM, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM
- $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$ mM, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 120$ mM
- $T = 310$ K, $z = +1$ for K⁺ and Na⁺, $z = -1$ for Cl⁻
- Permeability ratios: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ (at rest)

**Example A: Calculate $E_K$ (Equilibrium Potential for Potassium)**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{155}\right)$$

$\log_{10}(4/155) = \log_{10}(0.0258) = -1.588$

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.588) = -97.6 \text{ mV}$$

**Example B: Calculate $E_{Na}$ (Equilibrium Potential for Sodium)**

$$E_{Na} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right)$$

$\log_{10}(145/12) = \log_{10}(12.083) = 1.082$

$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 1.082 = +66.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Example C: Calculate $E_{Cl}$ (Equilibrium Potential for Chloride)**

$$E_{Cl} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{-1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{120}\right)$$

$\log_{10}(4/120) = \log_{10}(0.0333) = -1.477$

$$E_{Cl} = -61.5 \times (-1.477) = -90.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Example D: Calculate the Resting Membrane Potential Using the GHK Equation**

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{(1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1)(155) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(120)}\right)$$

Compute the numerator and denominator:
- Numerator: $4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$
- Denominator: $155 + 0.48 + 54 = 209.48$

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{11.6}{209.48}\right) = 61.5 \log_{10}(0.0554)$$

$\log_{10}(0.0554) = -1.257$

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.257) = -77.3 \text{ mV}$$

This calculated value of **–77.3 mV** is in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured RMP of mammalian neurons (≈ –70 mV), validating the ionic basis hypothesis.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** Always show the **logarithm calculation explicitly** in your answer. Examiners award partial marks for the setup even if the final arithmetic has minor errors.


```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Resting

    ActivatedNa --> InactivatedNa: Na+ channel inactivation
    InactivatedNa --> Resting: Repolarization completes
    Resting --> ActivatedK: Depolarization signal
    ActivatedK --> ClosedK: Membrane repolarized
    ClosedK --> Resting: Hyperpolarization ends
    note right of Resting: Vm = -70 mV
    note right of ActivatedNa: Vm rises to +30 mV
    note right of InactivatedNa: Absolute refractory period
    note right of ActivatedK: K+ efflux repolarizes cell
```

